criteria.name: Accurate
criteria.description: Every claim is either supported by the bill text or is accurate real-world context about the bill's subject (verifiable via general knowledge / web search). Nothing contradicts the bill text or the factual record.

criteria.name: No hallucinations
criteria.description: No fabricated or incorrect claims. Accurate context not stated in the bill — e.g. a program's common name, its administering agency, or the statute being amended — is acceptable and is NOT a hallucination. Only false or invented claims fail this criterion.

criteria.name: States purpose
criteria.description: The summary clearly identifies the bill's primary purpose or subject.

criteria.name: Changes to existing law
criteria.description: The summary describes how the bill amends, repeals, or adds to existing law (e.g. names the statute/program affected), when the bill does so.

criteria.name: Exceptions & conditions
criteria.description: The summary notes key exceptions, conditions, eligibility limits, or carve-outs that are material to the bill.

criteria.name: Effective dates & timing
criteria.description: The summary notes effective dates, sunsets, deadlines, or other timing provisions that the bill specifies.

criteria.name: Major provisions covered
criteria.description: Captures the bill's essential operative effect. A faithful high-level summary is fine; this fails only if a major operative provision is missing or misrepresented — not merely for omitting granular figures or minor sub-provisions.

criteria.name: Objective tone
criteria.description: The summary is neutral and non-partisan, with no advocacy, praise, criticism, or editorializing.

criteria.name: Coherent
criteria.description: The summary is well-organized, internally consistent, and reads clearly.

criteria.name: Concise
criteria.description: The summary avoids padding, repetition, boilerplate, and filler.

criteria.name: Correct entities
criteria.description: Any agencies, officials, programs, or statutes named — including accurate context beyond the bill text — are identified correctly. Adding a correct administering agency or program name is good; naming the wrong one fails.

criteria.name: Correct figures
criteria.description: Any dollar amounts, dates, or quantities the summary states are correct. Omitting figures does not fail this criterion; misstating one does.

bills.title: Relating to a national emergency declared by the President on March 13, 2020.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution terminates the national emergency that President declared on March 13, 2020, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The emergency was originally established through Proclamation 9994, published in the Federal Register at 85 Fed. Reg. 15337.

Acting under the authority of Section 202 of the National Emergencies Act (50 U.S.C. 1622), which allows Congress to end a national emergency by passing a joint resolution, this measure formally ends that declared emergency.

The resolution was passed by the House of Representatives on February 1, 2023, and by the Senate on March 29, 2023. It was approved and became Public Law 118-3 on April 10, 2023.

accurate.why: Termination, NEA sec 202, Proclamation 9994, dates all match; COVID context correct.

no_hallucinations.why: No fabricated claims; COVID/Proclamation 9994 are accurate context.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states it terminates the March 13, 2020 national emergency.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Notes it ends the emergency under NEA sec 202 (50 U.S.C. 1622).

exceptions_conditions.why: Bill has no material exceptions.

effective_dates.why: Gives passage and approval dates accurately.

major_provisions_covered.why: Captures the sole operative effect: termination.

correct_entities.why: NEA, Proclamation 9994, Public Law 118-3 all correct.

correct_figures.why: Fed. Reg. cite and all dates correct.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
This joint resolution terminates the national emergency declared by the President on March 13, 2020, in Proclamation 9994, as published at 85 Federal Register 15337.

The resolution acts under section 202 of the National Emergencies Act, which provides a mechanism for ending a national emergency. By operation of this law, the specified national emergency is ended. The resolution does not create a replacement emergency declaration, establish new programs, impose new requirements, or amend other statutory provisions beyond recording the termination of that emergency under the National Emergencies Act.

The law contains no stated exceptions, transition rules, or delayed implementation provisions. It was approved on April 10, 2023, and the termination applies pursuant to the enacted resolution.

states_purpose.why: States it terminates the declared emergency.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Notes action under NEA sec 202 and no amendments to other statutes.

exceptions_conditions.why: Correctly notes no exceptions exist.

concise.why: Slightly verbose on negatives but no real filler.

correct_entities.why: NEA, Proclamation 9994 correct.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Public Law 118-3, a joint resolution designated as H.J. Res. 7 of the 118th Congress, was enacted to terminate the national emergency originally declared by the President of the United States on March 13, 2020. 

Specifically, the resolution exercises authority under section 202 of the National Emergencies Act, which is codified at section 1622 of title 50, United States Code. By invoking this statutory provision, the joint resolution officially terminates the national emergency that was established under Proclamation 9994, published in volume 85 of the Federal Register at page 15337. 

The legislative history of the joint resolution indicates that it was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on February 1, 2023, and subsequently considered and passed by the Senate on March 29, 2023. The resolution officially became law upon receiving presidential approval on April 10, 2023. The text of the law does not contain any exceptions, conditions, or transitional periods, resulting in the immediate termination of the specified national emergency on the date of its approval.

accurate.why: All claims match; 'immediate termination on approval date' is reasonable.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Cites NEA sec 202 / 50 U.S.C. 1622.

exceptions_conditions.why: Notes no exceptions; none exist.

concise.why: Somewhat wordy for a one-line bill but acceptable.

correct_entities.why: PL 118-3, H.J.Res.7, NEA, Proclamation 9994 correct.

correct_figures.why: Vol. 85 Fed. Reg. 15337 and dates correct.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
Public Law 118-3 is a joint resolution enacted by the 118th United States Congress to terminate a national emergency previously declared by the President. The primary purpose of the law is to end the national emergency that was declared on March 13, 2020, through Proclamation 9994. 

To achieve this, the resolution invokes section 202 of the National Emergencies Act, codified at 50 U.S.C. 1622, which grants Congress the authority to terminate a national emergency. By enacting this resolution, Congress formally ends the emergency status that had been in place since March 2020. The text of the law does not specify any material exceptions, conditions, or phased deadlines; it provides for the direct and immediate termination of the declared emergency. 

The legislative history of the resolution indicates that it was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on February 1, 2023, and subsequently considered and passed by the Senate on March 29, 2023. The resolution was approved and signed into law on April 10, 2023. As a result of this enactment, the national emergency declared in Proclamation 9994 is terminated as of the date of approval.

bills.title: Disapproving the action of the District of Columbia Council in approving the Revised Criminal Code Act of 2022.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution disapproves of a measure passed by the District of Columbia Council, using Congress's authority to review District legislation under the District of Columbia Home Rule Act.

Specifically, the resolution expresses Congress's disapproval of the Revised Criminal Code Act of 2022 (D.C. Act 24-789), which the Council of the District of Columbia enacted on January 17, 2023, and transmitted to Congress on January 27, 2023, pursuant to section 602(c)(1) of the District of Columbia Home Rule Act.

Under the Home Rule Act, acts passed by the D.C. Council are subject to a congressional review period during which Congress may pass a joint resolution of disapproval. The enactment of such a resolution, when signed by the President, prevents the disapproved local act from taking effect. By this resolution, Congress blocks the Revised Criminal Code Act of 2022 from becoming law in the District of Columbia.

The resolution was passed by the House of Representatives on February 9, 2023, passed by the Senate on March 8, 2023, and approved on March 20, 2023, as Public Law 118-1.

The text of the resolution contains only the statement of disapproval and does not include additional provisions, conditions, or other changes to law.

accurate.why: All claims match the resolution and accurately describe Home Rule Act review mechanics.

no_hallucinations.why: No fabricated claims; the review-period context is correct.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states it disapproves the DC Revised Criminal Code Act.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Explains it blocks the DC act from taking effect under the Home Rule Act.

exceptions_conditions.why: No material exceptions or conditions in this resolution.

effective_dates.why: Correctly states House, Senate, and approval dates.

major_provisions_covered.why: Captures the sole operative effect: disapproval blocking the act.

concise.why: Somewhat padded with repeated explanation of the review mechanism.

correct_entities.why: DC Council, Home Rule Act, D.C. Act 24-789 all correct.

correct_figures.why: Dates and act numbers are correct.

The resolution’s sole operative provision states that Congress disapproves of the Council action approving that D.C. act. It does not amend the text of any federal statute, create new federal programs, impose reporting requirements, or include exceptions or conditions. google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Public Law 118-1, enacted as House Joint Resolution 26, is a joint resolution of Congress that disapproves of the Revised Criminal Code Act of 2022 (D.C. Act 24-789), which was enacted by the Council of the District of Columbia. 

The primary purpose of this legislation is to block the District of Columbia's proposed criminal code revisions from taking effect. Under section 602(c)(1) of the District of Columbia Home Rule Act, the D.C. Council is required to transmit enacted legislation to Congress for review. The D.C. Council enacted the Revised Criminal Code Act on January 17, 2023, and transmitted it to Congress on January 27, 2023. By passing this joint resolution, Congress formally declares its disapproval of the D.C. Council's action, preventing the local act from becoming law. 

The resolution was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on February 9, 2023, and by the Senate on March 8, 2023. It was officially approved and signed into law on March 20, 2023. The text of the resolution contains no material exceptions, conditions, or subsequent deadlines.

bills.title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of the Army, Corps of Engineers, Department of Defense and the Environmental Protection Agency relating to "Revised Definition of 'Waters of the United States'".

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution uses Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to disapprove and nullify a specific federal rule.

The rule in question is the "Revised Definition of 'Waters of the United States,'" which was jointly issued by the Department of the Army, Corps of Engineers, the Department of Defense, and the Environmental Protection Agency. This rule was published in the Federal Register at 88 Fed. Reg. 3004 on January 18, 2023, and it establishes how the term "waters of the United States" is defined for regulatory purposes (a definition that determines the scope of federal authority over various bodies of water).

Under the resolution, Congress formally disapproves this rule, and the rule "shall have no force or effect." In practical terms, if enacted, the resolution would block the rule from being implemented or enforced.

The document indicates that the resolution passed the House of Representatives on March 9, 2023, and was received in the Senate on March 14, 2023. The text itself contains no other provisions, exceptions, conditions, or effective dates beyond the disapproval of the identified rule.

accurate.why: Correctly describes CRA disapproval of the WOTUS rule; all claims match the text and context.

no_hallucinations.why: CRA/chapter 8 identification and rule purpose are accurate context, nothing fabricated.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states the resolution nullifies the WOTUS rule.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Explains the rule is given no force or effect.

exceptions_conditions.why: Resolution has no material exceptions.

effective_dates.why: No effective date beyond enactment; passage dates noted correctly.

concise.why: Slightly verbose but no real padding.

correct_entities.why: Agencies, CRA, Federal Register cite all correct.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary: This joint resolution nullifies a specific federal rule that redefined “waters of the United States” for regulatory purposes. The rule in question was issued jointly by the Department of the Army, Corps of Engineers, Department of Defense, and the Environmental Protection Agency and was published in the Federal Register on January 18, 2023. Under the resolution, Congress exercises its authority under the Congressional Review Act to disapprove the rule and declares that it shall have no force or effect. The resolution does not replace the rule with a new definition or direct the agencies to take any further action. It simply cancels the 2023 revised definition, which means the rule would be treated as if it had never taken effect. The resolution applies only to this specific rulemaking and does not amend the underlying Clean Water Act or other statutes. The measure passed the House of Representatives on March 9, 2023, and was received in the Senate on March 14, 2023.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
This joint resolution nullifies the rule titled Revised Definition of "Waters of the United States," which was submitted by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and the Environmental Protection Agency on January 18, 2023. The rule specifies which bodies of water fall under the scope of the Clean Water Act and are thereby under federal jurisdiction and protected. For example, the definition in the 2023 rule includes certain wetlands and ephemeral waters (e.g., waters that flow intermittently). 
 The 2023 rule replaced the 2020 Navigable Waters Protection Rule that included a narrower definition of waters of the United States.

This joint resolution exercises Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act to overturn a federal regulation issued by the executive branch.

Specifically, the resolution disapproves a rule submitted by the Department of Commerce titled "Procedures Covering Suspension of Liquidation, Duties and Estimated Duties in Accord With Presidential Proclamation 10414," which was published in the Federal Register at 87 Fed. Reg. 56868. Under the resolution, this rule "shall have no force or effect," meaning it would be nullified and treated as though it had never taken effect.

The Congressional Review Act allows Congress to disapprove agency rules through a joint resolution that, if enacted into law, prevents the rule from being implemented or enforced.

The text indicates that the resolution passed the House of Representatives on April 28, 2023, and was received in the Senate on May 1, 2023. For the resolution to take effect, it would need to be passed by the Senate and either signed by the President or enacted over a presidential veto.

The bill text itself does not describe the substantive content of the underlying Commerce Department rule beyond its title and Federal Register citation, nor does it specify additional conditions, exceptions, or effective dates.

bills.title: Disapproving the action of the District of Columbia Council in approving the Comprehensive Policing and Justice Reform Amendment Act of 2022.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution expresses Congress's disapproval of a law enacted by the District of Columbia. Specifically, it disapproves the Comprehensive Policing and Justice Reform Amendment Act of 2022 (D.C. Act 24-781), which was passed by the Council of the District of Columbia on January 19, 2023, and transmitted to Congress on January 26, 2023.

The resolution operates under the authority of the District of Columbia Home Rule Act, which establishes a process allowing Congress to review and disapprove laws enacted by the D.C. Council before they take effect. Under section 602(c)(1) of that Act, the D.C. Council is required to transmit its enacted acts to Congress, providing a window during which Congress may pass a joint resolution of disapproval.

The text of the resolution states that Congress disapproves of the Council's action in approving the referenced act. If enacted into law, this measure would block the D.C. policing and justice reform measure from taking effect, though the resolution itself does not describe the contents of the underlying D.C. act.

The resolution was passed by the House of Representatives on April 19, 2023. The text shown reflects the version received in the Senate on April 20, 2023, where it was read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs. For the resolution to have legal effect, it would require passage by the Senate and the signature of the President (or a veto override), consistent with the requirements for a joint resolution.

Ford Federal Direct Loan Program (published at 87 Fed. Reg. 61512 on October 12, 2022). The resolution references the rule as printed in the Congressional Record on March 22, 2023, along with a Government Accountability Office letter dated March 17, 2023, which concluded that the waivers and modifications constitute a "rule" subject to the Congressional Review Act.

Under the operative language, Congress disapproves this rule, and the resolution states that the rule "shall have no force or effect." This is the standard effect of a Congressional Review Act disapproval resolution: if enacted, it nullifies the agency action and prevents the rule from being implemented.

The document text indicates that the resolution passed the House of Representatives on May 24, 2023, and was received in the Senate on May 30, 2023. The resolution contains no additional provisions, exceptions, or separate effective dates; its sole function is to disapprove and void the identified student loan relief rule. To take effect, the resolution would need to be passed by the Senate and either signed by the President or enacted over a veto.

As its primary provision, the joint resolution dictates that the SEC's Staff Accounting Bulletin No. 121 shall have no force or effect. The House of Representatives passed the resolution on May 8, 2024, and it was received in the Senate the following day.

bills.title: Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act (Public Law 118-62), enacted May 13, 2024, bans the importation into the United States of certain low-enriched uranium connected to Russia.

The bill amends the USEC Privatization Act to prohibit, beginning 90 days after enactment, the importation of unirradiated low-enriched uranium that is produced in the Russian Federation or by a Russian entity. The prohibition also covers uranium determined to have been exchanged, swapped, or otherwise obtained in place of such Russian uranium in a manner designed to circumvent the restrictions. A "Russian entity" is defined as an entity organized under or subject to the jurisdiction of the Russian government.

The law allows the Secretary of Energy, in consultation with the Secretaries of State and Commerce, to waive the prohibition if the Secretary determines that no alternative viable source of low-enriched uranium is available to sustain operation of a nuclear reactor or U.S. nuclear energy company, or that importation is in the national interest. Imports allowed under a waiver are subject to annual quantity limits: 476,536 kilograms in 2024, 470,376 in 2025, 464,183 in 2026, and 459,083 in 2027. The Secretary of Commerce administers and enforces these limits in a manner imposing minimal burden on the commercial nuclear industry. Any waiver must terminate no later than January 1, 2028, and the Secretary of Energy must notify specified congressional committees when a waiver is issued, identifying the recipient.

The prohibition does not apply to imports by or under contract to the Department of Energy for national security or nonproliferation purposes, or to imports of non-uranium isotopes.

The prohibition provisions terminate on December 31, 2040. The bill also makes conforming amendments to the USEC Privatization Act, including striking and renumbering certain provisions; one related amendment takes effect 90 days after enactment.

bills.title: Preventing the Financing of Illegal Synthetic Drugs Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Preventing the Financing of Illegal Synthetic Drugs Act (Public Law 118-79) directs the Comptroller General of the United States (head of the Government Accountability Office) to conduct a study on illicit financing connected to the trafficking of synthetic drugs into the United States.

The bill begins with congressional findings citing statistics on drug overdose deaths, the growth in the number of synthetic substances, the profitability of fentanyl trafficking, and the role of financing in sustaining criminal organizations.

The study must examine illicit financing tied to trafficking of synthetic drugs, including fentanyl, methamphetamine, related substances, Captagon, and precursor chemicals. Specifically, the study is required to address: the business of synthetic drug trafficking and related illicit finance, including the involvement of transnational criminal organizations and terror syndicates and their trafficking corridors (source and transit countries); the business models these organizations use across the supply chain, from precursor purchase or production through manufacture, marketing, distribution, sales, and return of proceeds; the overlap between human trafficking and synthetic drug trafficking; the use of online drug markets and social media for marketing, sale, and payment; financial methods such as payment, money laundering, and repatriation of proceeds; the use of social media applications (such as Snapchat, Discord, and Facebook) and payment applications (such as CashApp) to facilitate transactions, particularly among young people; and U.S. government efforts to combat related illicit finance, including interagency, intergovernmental, and private-sector collaboration, as well as identified gaps or resource deficiencies.

The Comptroller General must issue a report to Congress containing all findings and determinations from the study no later than one year after the date of enactment.

The Act was approved September 13, 2024. It establishes a study and reporting requirement and does not, by its text, alter existing substantive law.

bills.title: Winnebago Land Transfer Act of 2023

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Winnebago Land Transfer Act of 2023 (Public Law 118-68) transfers certain federal lands into trust for the benefit of the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska.

The law declares specified federal lands to be part of the Winnebago Reservation that was created by the 1865 treaty between the United States and the Winnebago Tribe. It directs that all U.S. right, title, and interest in these lands—including improvements and appurtenances—be held in trust by the United States for the benefit of the Tribe, subject to the same terms and conditions as the lands described in the 1865 treaty. This transfer is subject to all valid existing rights.

The lands covered are three specific tracts identified by court case records: a portion of Tract No. 119 located in Woodbury County, Iowa (situated east of the Nebraska/Iowa state line and south of an extension of the Winnebago Reservation's northern boundary); Tract No. 210, as described in a declaration of taking filed in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Iowa; and Tract No. 113, as described in a judgment filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Nebraska.

The law includes one explicit restriction: it prohibits Class II and Class III gaming under the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act on any of the lands taken into trust under this Act at any time.

The Act was approved on July 12, 2024. It passed the House on February 5, 2024, and the Senate on June 20, 2024.

no_hallucinations.why: No fabricated claims; tract details accurate.

openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Winnebago Land Transfer Act of 2023 transfers specified federal lands into trust for the benefit of the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska and makes those lands part of the Winnebago Reservation.

The Act applies, subject to all valid existing rights, to all U.S. right, title, and interest, including improvements and appurtenances, in three described tracts of federal land. These include portions of Tract No. 119 in Woodbury County, Iowa, lying east of the Nebraska/Iowa state line and south of the easterly extension of the north line of the Winnebago Reservation; Tract No. 210 as described in a federal court declaration and judgment in the Northern District of Iowa; and Tract No. 113 as described in a federal court judgment in the District of Nebraska.

For these lands, the Act declares them to be part of the Winnebago Reservation created by the 1865 treaty between the United States and the Winnebago Tribe. It also requires that the United States hold the lands in trust for the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska under the same terms and conditions as lands described in that 1865 treaty.

The Act prohibits class II and class III gaming, as defined under the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act, on the lands taken into trust under the Act at any time. The Act was approved on July 12, 2024.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Winnebago Land Transfer Act of 2023 (Public Law 118-68), enacted on July 12, 2024, transfers administrative jurisdiction of certain federal lands to be held in trust for the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska. 

Under the Act, all right, title, and interest of the United States in specified federal lands—subject to any valid existing rights—are declared to be part of the Winnebago Reservation, which was originally established by an 1865 treaty. The United States will hold these lands in trust for the benefit of the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska, subject to the same terms and conditions as the lands described in the 1865 treaty. 

The affected federal lands include three specific parcels: 
* A portion of Tract No. 119 in Woodbury County, Iowa, lying east of the Nebraska-Iowa state line and south of the easterly extension of the Winnebago Reservation's north line; 
* Tract No. 210 in the Northern District of Iowa; and 
* Tract No. 113 in the District of Nebraska. 

As a major condition of this land transfer, the Act strictly prohibits Class II and Class III gaming under the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act from being conducted at any time on any of the lands taken into trust.

The Winnebago Land Transfer Actof 2023, approved on July 12, 2024, transfers administrative jurisdiction of specific federal lands from the Army Corps of Engineers to the Bureau of Indian Affairs to be held in trust for the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska. 

Under the Act, all right, title, and interest of the United States in the described federal lands are declared to be part of the Winnebago Reservation, originally created by the 1865 Treaty between the United States and the Winnebago Tribe. The lands will be held in trust by the United States for the benefit of the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska, subject to the same terms and conditions as those outlined in the 1865 Treaty. This transfer is subject to all valid existing rights.

The federal lands affected by this transfer include a specific portion of Tract No. 119 situated in Woodbury County, Iowa; Tract No. 210; and Tract No. 113. These tracts are legally described in prior court judgments and declarations of taking filed in the U.S. District Courts for the Northern District of Iowa and the District of Nebraska.

A material condition of the Act is a strict prohibition on gaming. Specifically, Class II and Class III gaming, as defined by the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act, is not allowed at any time on the lands taken into trust under this legislation.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The Winnebago Land Transfer Act of 2023 transfers certain federal lands from the administrative jurisdiction of the Army Corps of Engineers to the Bureau of Indian Affairs and places them into trust for the Winnebago Tribe of Nebraska. The Act declares that, subject to any valid existing rights, all right, title, and interest of the United States in the described lands—including improvements and appurtenances—become part of the Winnebago Reservation established by the 1865 Treaty between the United States and the Winnebago Tribe. These lands are to be held in trust under the same terms and conditions as those set out in that treaty.

The lands involved are three specific tracts located in Iowa and Nebraska, identified by references to court records: a portion of Tract No. 119 in Woodbury County, Iowa; Tract No. 210 as described in a federal district court case in Iowa; and Tract No. 113 as described in a federal district court case in Nebraska.

A key condition attached to the trust status is a permanent prohibition on gaming. Neither Class II nor Class III gaming, as defined by the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act, may be conducted on the lands taken into trust under this Act. The law took effect upon its approval on July 12, 2024.

bills.title: Dr. Emmanuel Bilirakis and Honorable Jennifer Wexton National Plan to End Parkinson’s Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Dr. Emmanuel Bilirakis and Honorable Jennifer Wexton National Plan to End Parkinson's Act" (Public Law 118-66) directs the Secretary of Health and Human Services to establish a National Parkinson's Project aimed at preventing, diagnosing, treating, and curing Parkinson's disease. The law amends the Public Health Service Act by adding a new part addressing Parkinson's and related disorders. For purposes of the law, "Parkinson's" includes Parkinson's disease and other neurodegenerative Parkinsonisms such as multiple system atrophy, corticobasal degeneration, progressive supranuclear palsy, and Parkinson's-related dementia.

Under the Project, the Secretary must create and periodically update an integrated national plan; estimate current federal investment and any additional investment needed; coordinate research and services across federal agencies; encourage development of treatments; improve early diagnosis and care coordination; review the disease's effects on patients, caregivers, and families; coordinate with international bodies; and collaborate to avoid duplicating research. Beginning no later than 24 months after enactment and annually thereafter, the Secretary must assess the nation's progress and provide recommendations for priority actions.

The law establishes an Advisory Council on Parkinson's Research, Care, and Services. The Council includes federal members appointed from specified agencies (including the CDC, NIH, CMS, FDA, the Department of Veterans Affairs, the Department of Defense, and others) and 10 non-federal expert members, including patient advocates (at least one with young-onset Parkinson's), a family caregiver, health care providers, researchers, and nonprofit representatives. The Council must meet at least quarterly for its first two years, convene an annual research meeting (beginning within 24 months of enactment), hold open meetings, and submit annual reports to the Secretary and Congress beginning within 18 months of enactment. These reports must catalog federally funded efforts and offer recommendations on funding, patient costs, health outcomes, care quality, and research, including environmental triggers.

Federal agencies must share relevant Parkinson's information with the Secretary, consistent with disclosure laws. The Advisory Council terminates, and the section ceases to be effective, at the end of calendar year 2035. The Act was approved July 2, 2024.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Act establishes a National Parkinson’s Project within the Public Health Service Act, directed by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, to prevent, diagnose, treat, and cure Parkinson’s disease and related neurodegenerative Parkinsonisms, including multiple system atrophy, corticobasal degeneration, progressive supranuclear palsy, and Parkinson’s-related dementia.

The Secretary must create, maintain, and periodically update an integrated national plan addressing prevention, diagnosis, treatment, symptom reduction, and slowing or stopping disease progression. The Secretary must also assess current federal investment in Parkinson’s-related work, estimate additional needed investment when applicable, coordinate research and services across federal agencies, encourage development of treatments and strategies, improve early diagnosis and care coordination, review impacts on patients, caregivers, and families, coordinate with international bodies when possible, and collaborate to avoid duplicative research where practicable.

Within 24 months of enactment, and annually thereafter, the Secretary must assess national progress in preparing for and responding to the burden of Parkinson’s and include priority recommendations, implementation steps, and other appropriate items.

The Act also requires the Secretary to establish an Advisory Council on Parkinson’s Research, Care, and Services. The council must include representatives from specified federal agencies and 10 non-federal experts, including patient advocates, a caregiver, health care and research specialists, and nonprofit representatives, with attention to minority, disproportionately affected, and underrepresented communities. The council must meet quarterly for its first two years, then at the Secretary’s discretion, and must hold an annual public research meeting beginning within 24 months. Beginning within 18 months and annually thereafter, it must report to the Secretary and Congress on federally funded Parkinson’s efforts, recommendations, priority actions, evaluation of the national plan, and implementation steps.

Federal agencies with Parkinson’s-related information must share it with the Secretary as needed, consistent with disclosure laws. The Advisory Council and the new statutory section terminate at the end of calendar year 2035.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The "Dr. Emmanuel Bilirakis and Honorable Jennifer Wexton National Plan to End Parkinson's Act" (Public Law 118-66) amends the Public Health Service Act to direct the Secretary of Health and Human Services (HHS) to establish and carry out the National Parkinson's Project. The project's primary purpose is to prevent, diagnose, treat, and cure Parkinson's, which the bill defines to include Parkinson's disease and other neurodegenerative Parkinsonisms, such as multiple system atrophy, progressive supranuclear palsy, and Parkinson's-related dementia.

Under the Act, the Secretary must create and maintain an integrated national plan to address Parkinson's, coordinate related research and services across federal agencies, and encourage the development of safe and effective treatments. Additionally, the Secretary must conduct an annual assessment of the nation's progress in managing the disease, with the first assessment due within 24 months of enactment. 

The bill also establishes the Advisory Council on Parkinson's Research, Care, and Services, which will consist of appointed federal officials and ten non-federal experts, including patient advocates, caregivers, health care providers, and researchers. The Advisory Council is required to meet quarterly for its first two years, host an annual public research meeting starting within 24 months of enactment, and submit an annual report to Congress and the Secretary beginning within 18 months of enactment. Federal agencies are required to share relevant information with the Secretary to support these reports. Both the Advisory Council and the provisions of this Act are scheduled to terminate at the end of calendar year 2035.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This law creates a national initiative called the National Parkinson’s Project, led by the Secretary of Health and Human Services. Its purpose is to prevent, diagnose, treat, and cure Parkinson’s disease and related neurodegenerative disorders, including multiple system atrophy, corticobasal degeneration, progressive supranuclear palsy, and Parkinson’s-related dementia.

The Project requires the Secretary to develop and periodically update an integrated national plan aimed at preventing and treating Parkinson’s, slowing or stopping its progression, and ameliorating symptoms. The Secretary must conduct an annual assessment of the nation’s progress in addressing the growing burden of Parkinson’s, beginning within 24 months of enactment. This assessment must include priority action recommendations and a description of steps taken or planned to implement them. The Secretary must also provide estimates of current federal investment in Parkinson’s efforts and, where applicable, the investment needed. Additional duties include coordinating research and services across federal agencies, encouraging development of safe and effective treatments, improving early diagnosis and care coordination, reviewing the impact on patients and caregivers, and collaborating internationally while avoiding duplication with existing research on related disorders.

The law establishes an Advisory Council on Parkinson’s Research, Care, and Services, composed of federal experts from specified agencies—such as the National Institutes of Health, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the Department of Veterans Affairs—and ten non-federal members, including patient advocates, a caregiver, health care providers, researchers, and nonprofit representatives. The Council must meet at least quarterly for its first two years, hold an annual research meeting open to the public, and submit an annual report to the Secretary and Congress starting within 18 months of enactment. The report must list federally funded Parkinson’s efforts, recommend priority actions, and evaluate the national plan’s implementation. Federal agencies must share relevant Parkinson’s information with the Secretary to support this report. Both the Advisory Council and the entire section sunset at the end of calendar year 2035.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Dr. Emmanuel Bilirakis and Honorable Jennifer Wexton National Plan to End Parkinson's Act
This act requires the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) to carry out a project to address Parkinson's disease (a progressive brain disorder that causes unintended or uncontrollable movements) and related conditions.
Among other components of the project, HHS must (1) implement and periodically update a national plan to coordinate and guide efforts to prevent, diagnose, treat, and cure the disease; and (2) improve the care of those with the disease. HHS must also annually assess the preparations for and response to the increased burden of Parkinson's disease.
In addition, the act establishes a council, comprised of federal and nonfederal stakeholders, to advise HHS on and make recommendations concerning the prevention and treatment of Parkinson's disease.
The provisions of the act terminate at the end of calendar year 2035.

bills.title: STOP CCP Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Sanctioning Tyrannical and Oppressive People within the Chinese Communist Party Act" (STOP CCP Act) would require the President to impose sanctions on certain members of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) and related parties in response to actions by the People's Republic of China (PRC).

**Who would be sanctioned.** Within 30 days of enactment, the President must impose sanctions on: members (including alternates) of the CCP's Central Committee, or any successor body, who engage in specified conduct; adult family members of those individuals (including spouses and their adult relatives); and Chinese state-owned enterprises that the President determines are illegally manufacturing or distributing fentanyl or its precursor chemicals.

**Sanctionable conduct.** A Central Committee member is covered if they play a significant role in developing or implementing policies the President determines appear designed to: violate Hong Kong's autonomy; intimidate or increase aggression toward Taiwan; contribute to political oppression or human rights violations within the PRC (including against Uyghur Muslims); or knowingly engage in malicious activities, such as disinformation campaigns, to interfere with U.S. elections.

**Sanctions imposed.** Sanctions include blocking the person's property and interests in property in the United States under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), and barring covered aliens from receiving visas or entering the United States, including revocation of existing visas.

**Exceptions and limits.** Sanctions do not apply where needed to comply with the UN Headquarters Agreement or to authorized U.S. intelligence, law enforcement, or national security activities. The Act does not authorize sanctions on the importation of goods. IEEPA penalties apply to violators.

**Waiver and termination.** The President may waive sanctions in 60-day increments by certifying to Congress that doing so is vital to national security. Sanctions may be terminated upon certification that the PRC has ceased specified conduct (regarding Uyghurs, Tibet, Taiwan, Hong Kong, and intellectual property theft), or that a sanctioned person has renounced CCP membership and remediated their conduct. Waiver and license authority terminates two years after enactment.

The bill passed the House on September 25, 2024.

accurate.why: All claims match: who is sanctioned, conduct, sanctions, exceptions, waiver/termination.

no_hallucinations.why: IEEPA naming and details are accurate context.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states purpose to mandate sanctions on CCP members and related parties.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Bill creates new sanctions authority rather than amending existing law.

exceptions_conditions.why: Covers UN Headquarters, intel/LE/national security, and goods-importation exceptions.

effective_dates.why: Notes 30-day deadline, 60-day waivers, 2-year sunset of waiver authority.

major_provisions_covered.why: Comprehensive coverage of all operative provisions.

correct_entities.why: IEEPA, committees, Central Committee all correct.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The bill’s primary purpose is to require sanctions on certain Chinese Communist Party officials, their adult family members, and certain Chinese state-owned enterprises connected to illegal fentanyl production or distribution.

Within 30 days of enactment, the President must impose sanctions on members, including alternate members, of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party, or any successor organization if the Central Committee is dissolved, if the President determines they play a significant role in developing or implementing policies or laws that appear designed to violate Hong Kong’s autonomy; harass, intimidate, or increase aggression toward the people of Taiwan; contribute to political oppression or human rights violations in China, including against Uyghur Muslims; or knowingly engage in malicious activities, including online disinformation and propaganda, to interfere with U.S. federal, state, or local elections. The sanctions also apply to adult family members, including spouses and adult family members of spouses, of covered officials, and to Chinese state-owned enterprises that illegally manufacture or distribute fentanyl or knowingly or intentionally manufacture or distribute fentanyl precursor chemicals for illegal fentanyl production or distribution.

Required sanctions include blocking property and interests in property under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act when within U.S. jurisdiction or control of a U.S. person, and making covered aliens inadmissible, ineligible for visas or other entry documents, and ineligible for immigration benefits. Existing visas or entry documents must be revoked immediately. The bill applies IEEPA civil and criminal penalties to violations and requires implementing regulations within 30 days.

Exceptions apply for U.S. obligations under the United Nations Headquarters Agreement or other international obligations, authorized U.S. intelligence, law enforcement, or national security activities, and the importation of goods. The President may waive sanctions for renewable periods of up to 60 days if certified to Congress as vital to U.S. national security, with 15 days’ advance notice. Sanctions may be terminated after specified certifications to Congress and public notice. The President’s authority to issue waivers or licenses ends two years after enactment.

accurate.why: Accurate detailed description including 15-day notice and regulations within 30 days.

changes_to_existing_law.why: New authority, not an amendment.

exceptions_conditions.why: Covers all three exceptions and waiver conditions.

effective_dates.why: Notes 30 days, 60-day waivers with 15-day notice, 2-year sunset.

correct_entities.why: IEEPA correct.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The "Sanctioning Tyrannical and Oppressive People within the Chinese Communist Party Act" (STOP CCP Act) requires the President to impose sanctions on members of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) and related entities. Within 30 days of enactment, the President must block U.S.-based property transactions and restrict U.S. entry—including revoking existing visas—for Central Committee members (and successor organizations) determined to have played a significant role in violating Hong Kong's autonomy, aggressing against Taiwan, violating human rights in China (including of Uyghur Muslims), or interfering in U.S. elections. These sanctions also apply to their adult family members and spouses, as well as Chinese state-owned enterprises that manufacture or distribute fentanyl or its precursor chemicals.

The bill exempts authorized U.S. intelligence, law enforcement, and national security activities, as well as admissions required under the United Nations Headquarters Agreement. Sanctions do not apply to the importation of goods.

The President must issue implementing regulations within 30 days of enactment. The President may waive sanctions for up to 60 days if vital to U.S. national security, though this waiver and licensing authority expires two years after enactment. Sanctions may be terminated if the President certifies that China has ceased its genocide of Uyghurs, stopped aggression against Taiwan, respected Hong Kong's autonomy, ceased Tibetan oppression, and ended intellectual property theft. Alternatively, individual sanctions can be lifted if the person renounces CCP membership and remediates their conduct, or if the enterprise halts fentanyl operations.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The bill requires the President to impose sanctions on certain members of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) and related individuals and entities. Within 30 days of enactment, the President must block the property and revoke the U.S. visas of members of the CCP Central Committee, including alternate members, whom the President determines play a significant role in developing or implementing policies designed to violate Hong Kong's autonomy, increase aggression toward Taiwan, contribute to human rights violations within China (including against Uyghur Muslims), or interfere with U.S. elections. Sanctions also apply to adult family members of these individuals, members of any successor organization to the Central Committee, and Chinese state-owned enterprises involved in the illegal manufacture or distribution of fentanyl or its precursor chemicals.

Sanctions involve blocking property transactions under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act and rendering targeted aliens ineligible for U.S. visas, admission, or parole, with existing visas revoked immediately. 

The bill includes several exceptions. Sanctions do not apply to actions necessary for U.S. compliance with the United Nations Headquarters Agreement, authorized U.S. intelligence or law enforcement activities, or the importation of goods. The President may waive sanctions for up to 60 days if certifying to Congress at least 15 days in advance that the waiver is vital to U.S. national security; this waiver authority expires two years after enactment. The President may terminate sanctions after providing a 15-day prior notice to Congress and publishing the certification, either if the PRC and CCP cease specified actions—including ending the genocide of Uyghurs, ceasing oppression of Tibetans, stopping aggression toward Taiwan, restoring Hong Kong's autonomy, and ending intellectual property theft—or if the sanctioned individual renounces CCP membership and remediates their conduct, or if a sanctioned enterprise ceases fentanyl-related activities.

This bill, titled the “Sanctioning Tyrannical and Oppressive People within the Chinese Communist Party Act” or the “STOP CCP Act,” requires the President to impose sanctions on certain members of the Chinese Communist Party and related entities. Its primary purpose is to penalize individuals the President determines are responsible for specific actions by the People’s Republic of China.

Within 30 days of enactment, the President must impose sanctions on: each member and alternate member of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party (or any successor organization) who the President determines plays a significant role in developing or implementing policies designed to violate Hong Kong’s autonomy; harass, intimidate, or increase aggression toward Taiwan; contribute to political oppression or human rights violations within China, including against Uyghur Muslims; or willfully engage in malicious activities, such as online disinformation campaigns, to interfere in U.S. elections. Sanctions are also required for adult family members of those individuals and for Chinese state-owned enterprises the President determines are illegally manufacturing or distributing fentanyl or knowingly manufacturing or distributing fentanyl precursor chemicals for illegal use.

The mandated sanctions consist of blocking all U.S.-based property and interests in property of the designated persons, and making them ineligible for U.S. visas, admission, or parole, while revoking any current visas. Exceptions exist for compliance with the United Nations Headquarters Agreement and for authorized U.S. intelligence, law enforcement, and national security activities. The authority to impose sanctions does not extend to restricting the importation of goods.

The President may waive sanctions for renewable 60-day periods by certifying to Congress that the waiver is vital to U.S. national security interests. Sanctions can be terminated if the President certifies that China has ceased specific actions, including the genocide of Uyghur Muslims, oppression of Tibetans, threats and aggression toward Taiwan, undermining of Hong Kong’s autonomy, and intellectual property theft; or if an individual sanctioned person has renounced party membership and denounced the relevant conduct, or a sanctioned enterprise has ceased the illicit fentanyl-related activities. The President’s authority to issue waivers or licenses for these sanctions expires two years after enactment.

accurate.why: Accurate across sanctions, exceptions, waiver, and termination.

changes_to_existing_law.why: New authority.

exceptions_conditions.why: Covers exceptions and termination conditions.

correct_entities.why: Entities correct.

bills.title: Restoring Benefits to Defrauded Veterans Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Restoring Benefits to Defrauded Veterans Act (Public Law 118-114) amends title 38 of the United States Code to address situations in which a beneficiary of veterans' benefits dies before receiving a repayment owed to them for benefits that were misused by their fiduciary.

Under existing law (section 6107 of title 38), the Secretary of Veterans Affairs is required to repay beneficiaries whose benefits were misused by a fiduciary appointed to manage them. This bill adds a new provision specifying what happens when such a beneficiary dies before receiving that repayment.

The new subsection (c) directs the Secretary to pay the owed benefits, in the case of a deceased beneficiary, to an individual or entity in accordance with section 5121 of title 38 (the provision governing payment of benefits due but unpaid at the time of a beneficiary's death). The law explicitly prohibits the Secretary from making such a payment to a fiduciary who misused the beneficiary's benefits.

To accommodate this addition, the bill makes technical changes: it redesignates the existing subsections (c) and (d) as (d) and (e), and updates a cross-reference in the redesignated subsection (e) so that it refers to the entire section rather than only subsections (a) or (b).

The Act was approved on November 25, 2024. It contains no separate effective date or deadline provisions.

exceptions_conditions.why: notes bar on paying a fiduciary who misused benefits

This Act adds a new provision requiring that, if the beneficiary dies before a payment is made under those reissuance authorities, the Secretary must pay the benefits to an individual or entity in accordance with section 5121 of title 38, which governs payment of certain accrued benefits after a beneficiary’s death.

The Act includes an exception: the Secretary may not make such a payment to a fiduciary who misused the beneficiary’s benefits. It also makes conforming organizational and wording changes to section 6107 so that its provisions apply to the section as amended.

The Act does not establish a separate deadline or effective date beyond its enactment.

bills.title: Maternal and Child Health Stillbirth Prevention Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Maternal and Child Health Stillbirth Prevention Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-69) amends title V of the Social Security Act to clarify that federal Maternal and Child Health Services Block Grant funds may be used for stillbirth prevention and related research.

The bill makes two changes to Section 501(a) of the Social Security Act. First, it adds language to the section describing the purposes of block grant funding, specifying that funds may be used "to reduce the incidence of stillbirth" as part of efforts to improve the health of mothers and children.

Second, it expands the list of permissible activities to include evidence-based programs and outcome research aimed at reducing stillbirths. The bill provides examples of such activities, including: tracking and raising awareness of fetal movements; improving birth timing for pregnancies with risk factors; encouraging safe sleeping positions during pregnancy; screening and surveillance for fetal growth restriction; supporting smoking cessation during pregnancy; community-based programs offering home visits or other support; and any other research or evidence-based programming to prevent stillbirths.

The Act does not create new funding but clarifies that existing block grant funds may be directed toward these stillbirth prevention purposes. It was approved on July 12, 2024.

bills.title: FISHES Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The FISHES Act (Fishery Improvement to Streamline untimely regulatory Hurdles post Emergency Situation Act), enacted as Public Law 118-229 on January 4, 2025, amends the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act to establish deadlines for processing "spend plans" tied to fishery resource disaster funding.

Under the amended law, a requester that has received an affirmative fishery resource disaster determination must, in order to obtain an allocation of available funds, submit a spend plan to the Secretary within 120 days after being notified that funds are available. The spend plan must include, where applicable: objectives and outcomes (emphasizing the factors contributing to the disaster and minimizing future uninsured losses), a statement of work, and budget details.

The bill sets timelines for review. The Secretary must review each submitted spend plan to determine whether it is complete and provide notice within 10 days. If the Secretary finds a plan incomplete, that notice must include a detailed description of the information needed to make the plan complete.

The bill also revises the funding availability provision. Funds must be made available to grantees no later than 90 days after the Secretary receives a complete spend plan. If requested, the Director of the Office of Management and Budget may review a completed spend plan at the same time as the Secretary, but the Director may not delay the 90-day timeline.

The Act took effect upon its approval on January 4, 2025.

changes_to_existing_law.why: describes amendment to Magnuson-Stevens Act sec. 312(a)(6)

exceptions_conditions.why: notes spend-plan completeness condition and OMB no-delay limit

correct_entities.why: Magnuson-Stevens Act, Secretary (of Commerce), OMB correct

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The FISHES Act amends the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act to establish specific timelines and requirements for spend plans related to fishery resource disaster determinations. 

Under the Act, a requester with an affirmative fishery resource disaster determination must submit a spend plan to the Secretary within 120 days of receiving notification that funds are available. The spend plan must include, if applicable, objectives and outcomes emphasizing the factors contributing to the disaster and minimizing future uninsured losses, a statement of work, and budget details.

The Secretary is required to review the submitted spend plan to determine if it is complete and provide notice within 10 days. If the Secretary determines the plan is incomplete, the notice must include a detailed description of the information necessary to make the plan complete.

Once a complete spend plan is received, funds must be made available to grantees no later than 90 days after that date. Additionally, if requested, the Director of the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) may review a completed spend plan concurrently with the Secretary. However, the OMB Director is prohibited from delaying the 90-day timeline for making funds available to grantees. The Act was approved on January 4, 2025.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The FISHES Act (Fishery Improvement to Streamline untimely regulatory Hurdles post Emergency Situation Act) amends the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act to set binding deadlines for the review and release of federal funds following a fishery resource disaster. Under the new law, a requester with an affirmative disaster determination must submit a spend plan to the Secretary within 120 days of being notified that funds are available. The plan must include, where applicable, objectives and outcomes, a statement of work, and budget details. The Secretary must review the plan within 10 days to determine whether it is complete. If the plan is incomplete, the Secretary must provide a detailed description of the information needed to complete it.

Once a complete spend plan is received, funds must be made available to grantees no later than 90 days later. The Director of the Office of Management and Budget may review the completed spend plan at the same time as the Secretary but is explicitly prohibited from delaying that 90-day disbursement deadline. These provisions replace prior language that did not impose such strict timelines, aiming to reduce administrative delays in delivering disaster assistance to fishing communities. The Act took effect upon its approval on January 4, 2025.

Fishery Improvement to Streamline untimely regulatory Hurdles post Emergency Situation Act or the FISHES Act
This bill establishes procedures for the review of spend plans submitted to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) by requesters seeking fishery resource disaster assistance funding.
The bill requires NOAA to review a spend plan within 10 days of its submission and determine whether it is complete. If such spend plan is not complete, NOAA must provide the requester (i.e., prospective grantee) with a detailed description of the information that is necessary for the spend plan to be determined complete. Further, NOAA must notify a requester when the spend plan has been determined to be complete. 
Under current law, fishery resource disaster assistance funds must be disbursed to approved grantees within 90 days after NOAA has received a completed spend plan. The bill provides that the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) may review a completed spend plan concurrently with NOAA, provided OMB's review does not delay the 90-day timeline for providing funds to the grantee.

bills.title: Michel O. Maceda Memorial Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This bill, the "Michel O. Maceda Memorial Act" (Public Law 118-139), designates a U.S. Customs and Border Protection facility in honor of a fallen agent.

The bill's primary purpose is to officially name the Air and Marine Operations Marine Unit of U.S. Customs and Border Protection located at 101 Km 18.5 in Cabo Rojo, Puerto Rico, as the "Michel O. Maceda Marine Unit."

The bill includes findings about Michel O. Maceda, stating that he served in the U.S. Army as a wheeled vehicle mechanic before joining the U.S. Border Patrol as an agent in Douglas, Arizona, in April 2016. He became a Marine Interdiction Agent with U.S. Customs and Border Protection, Air and Marine Operations, in Mayaguez, Puerto Rico, in 2021. According to the findings, on November 17, 2022, Agent Maceda and two other agents were conducting a drug interdiction stop off the coast of Puerto Rico when Maceda was fatally shot by an occupant of the boarded vessel. He is survived by his daughter, brother, and parents.

The bill provides that the marine unit shall be known and designated by the new name, and it specifies that any reference in any U.S. law, map, regulation, document, paper, or other record to that marine unit shall be considered a reference to the "Michel O. Maceda Marine Unit."

The Act was approved on December 11, 2024.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The "Michel O. Maceda Memorial Act" (Public Law 118-139), approved on December 11, 2024, is a federal statute enacted to honor the memory of Marine Interdiction Agent Michel O. Maceda by naming a federal facility in Puerto Rico after him. 

The primary provision of the Act officially designates the Air and Marine Operations Marine Unit of the U.S. Customs and Border Protection, located at 101 Km 18.5 in Cabo Rojo, Puerto Rico, as the "Michel O. Maceda Marine Unit." To implement this change, the law mandates that any reference to this specific marine unit in any United States law, map, regulation, document, paper, or other official record must be deemed a reference to the "Michel O. Maceda Marine Unit."

The Act includes congressional findings that detail Agent Maceda’s service history to provide context for the designation. According to the text, Agent Maceda served in the United States Army as a wheeled vehicle mechanic before joining the United States Border Patrol as an agent in Douglas, Arizona, in April 2016. In May 2021, he became a Marine Interdiction Agent with U.S. Customs and Border Protection in Mayaguez, Puerto Rico. On November 17, 2022, Agent Maceda was mortally wounded when an occupant of a target vessel shot him and two other agents during a drug interdiction stop and boarding operation off the coast of Puerto Rico. He is survived by his daughter, brother, and parents.

The Act does not contain any material exceptions, conditions, or future implementation deadlines, and it went into effect upon its approval on December 11, 2024.

bills.title: AACE Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Accelerating Appraisals and Conservation Efforts Act (AACE Act), Public Law 118-230, establishes a policy on appraisal and valuation services for real property in transactions under the jurisdiction of the Secretary of the Interior.

The bill's central provision allows a "covered appraiser" (a state-licensed or state-certified real property appraiser) to be licensed or certified in only one state in order to perform appraisal or valuation services for real property in any state, provided the work is for a transaction over which the Secretary of the Interior has jurisdiction. This changes prior law: any federal or state law enacted before this Act that requires an appraiser to be licensed in a specific state to work in that state no longer applies to such transactions. Outside of these transactions, existing licensing requirements continue to apply, and the Act does not otherwise alter the application of federal or state laws to non-federal appraisers.

When the Secretary uses a non-federal appraiser, the Secretary must, to the maximum extent practicable, use a certified general real property appraiser licensed in the state where the property is located. An appraiser licensed in another state may be used only if the Secretary determines that an in-state appraiser is unavailable within a reasonable time, not qualified for the assignment, or not cost competitive.

The Act requires the Secretary to submit a report to Congress within one year of enactment and annually for at least four more years. Each report must compare the number of appraisal requests and completion times before and after enactment, assess the impact of the new flexibility, offer recommendations, provide an overview of the Department's appraisal workforce, and list all cases where this authority was used and the criteria applied.

Within 90 days of enactment, the Department must make publicly available online all policies, manuals, guidance, handbooks, and procedures related to scoping, contracting, and reviewing appraisals.

The Act was approved January 4, 2025.

accurate.why: Tracks the one-state-license rule, in-state preference, reporting, and public-availability provisions.

states_purpose.why: States the bill eases licensing for Interior real-property appraisals.

The Accelerating Appraisals and Conservation Efforts Act establishes rules for appraisal and valuation services for real property transactions under the jurisdiction of the Secretary of the Interior.

The Act provides that a state-licensed or state-certified real property appraiser needs to be licensed or certified in only one state to perform appraisal or valuation services in any state for a transaction over which the Secretary has jurisdiction. It limits the effect of preexisting federal or state laws or regulations that would otherwise require an appraiser to be licensed or certified in the particular state where the property is located, but only for such Interior Department transactions. The Act does not otherwise change the application of federal or state laws and regulations to non-federal appraisers, and it does not authorize appraisers to work outside their licensed state except for transactions under the Secretary’s jurisdiction.

When the Secretary uses a non-federal appraiser, the Secretary must, to the maximum extent practicable, use a certified general real property appraiser licensed in the state where the property is located. The Secretary may use a certified general appraiser licensed in another state only if an in-state appraiser is determined to be unavailable within a reasonable time, not qualified for the assignment, or not cost competitive.

Within one year after enactment, and annually for at least the next four years, the Secretary must report to Congress on appraisal and valuation request volumes and completion times, the impact of the new licensing flexibility, any recommended congressional actions, workforce and contractor information, and cases in which the Act’s authority was used.

Within 90 days after enactment, the Department of the Interior must make publicly available online, and provide upon request, all policies and related materials governing the scoping, contracting, and review of appraisals performed for the Department.

This bill relaxes requirements for the Department of the Interior in appraising and valuing real property for transactions over which Interior has jurisdiction.

bills.title: To restore the ability of the people of American Samoa to approve amendments to the territorial constitution based on majority rule in a democratic act of self-determination, as authorized pursuant to an Act of Congress delegating administration of Federal territorial law in the territory to the President, and to the Secretary of the Interior under Executive Order 10264, dated June 29, 1951, under which the Constitution of American Samoa was approved and may be amended without requirement for further congressional action, subject to the authority of Congress under the Territorial Clause in article IV, section 3, clause 2 of the United States Constitution.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This law repeals an existing restriction on how the Constitution of American Samoa may be amended.

Specifically, the Act repeals Section 12 of Public Law 98-213 (codified at 48 U.S.C. 1662a). Under that prior provision, amendments to or modifications of the American Samoa constitution were subject to a restriction requiring congressional action. By removing this provision, the law restores the process under which the American Samoa constitution—originally approved and amendable through authority delegated to the President and the Secretary of the Interior pursuant to Executive Order 10264 (dated June 29, 1951)—may be amended without requiring further action by Congress. As stated in the bill's title, the stated aim is to allow the people of American Samoa to approve constitutional amendments based on majority rule as an act of self-determination, while noting that this remains subject to Congress's authority under the Territorial Clause (Article IV, Section 3, Clause 2 of the U.S. Constitution).

The Act consists of a single operative provision and contains no additional conditions or exceptions. It was approved and became effective on January 4, 2025.

Its primary purpose is to restore the ability of the people of American Samoa to approve amendments to the territorial constitution through majority rule as an act of democratic self-determination, without requiring further congressional action for such amendments to take effect.

The law repeals section 12 of Public Law 98-213, codified at 48 U.S.C. 1662a. By repealing that provision, it eliminates the restriction it imposed on amendments or modifications to the Constitution of American Samoa. The act recognizes that the administration of federal territorial law in American Samoa was delegated by Congress to the President and, under Executive Order 10264, to the Secretary of the Interior, under whose authority the Constitution of American Samoa was approved and may be amended.

The law does not create a new amendment procedure, set deadlines, or include exceptions. It remains subject to Congress’s authority over territories under the Territorial Clause of the U.S. Constitution. The repeal was enacted on January 4, 2025.

Public Law 118-232, enacted on January 4, 2025, aims to restore the ability of the people of American Samoa to approve amendments to their territorial constitution through majority rule in a democratic act of self-determination. 

To achieve this purpose, the Act repeals Section 12 of Public Law 98-213 (codified at 48 U.S.C. 1662a). This repeal removes the previous federal restriction on amending or modifying the Constitution of American Samoa, thereby allowing the territorial constitution to be amended without the requirement of further congressional action. 

This change aligns with the administrative framework established under Executive Order 10264, dated June 29, 1951, which delegated the administration of federal territorial law in American Samoa to the President and the Secretary of the Interior. Under this authority, the Constitution of American Samoa was originally approved. While the Act eliminates the requirement for prior congressional approval for constitutional amendments, all such amendments remain subject to the ultimate authority of Congress under the Territorial Clause of the United States Constitution (Article IV, Section 3, Clause 2).

bills.title: Think Differently About Emergencies Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Think Differently About Emergencies Act" (Public Law 118-123), enacted November 25, 2024, directs the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) to report to Congress and conduct a review concerning the assistance it provides to people with disabilities and their families affected by major disasters.

The Act has two main requirements. First, within 120 days of enactment, the FEMA Administrator must submit a report to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure and the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs. The report must describe the assistance and resources FEMA provides—to states, localities, nonprofit organizations, and directly to individuals—for people with intellectual and developmental disabilities and their families who are impacted by major disasters declared under section 401 of the Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act. The report must include the resources and best practices FEMA makes available, the process for training and deploying personnel to coordinate disaster assistance, the number of relevant FEMA programs (including those providing accommodations), FEMA's outreach to state, local, and nonprofit experts, and identified areas for improvement.

Second, within one year of enactment, both the Comptroller General of the United States and the FEMA Administrator must each conduct a review of the assistance and resources FEMA provides to states, localities, nonprofit organizations, and individuals to help people with intellectual and developmental disabilities, people with physical disabilities, and their families. Each review must identify potential legal impediments to providing such assistance, and provide Congress with recommendations on how FEMA can improve the assistance, enhance coordination with states, localities, and nonprofit organizations, and streamline the application and delivery process. Each must also recommend any legislative reforms needed.

The Act establishes reporting and review obligations only; it does not, by its terms, create new assistance programs or alter the substance of existing disaster relief law.

Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act. The report must describe available resources and best practices; explain how FEMA trains and deploys personnel to coordinate with states, localities, and nonprofits after disasters; list FEMA-administered programs that support disaster preparedness and recovery for these individuals, including accommodations; describe outreach to state, local, and nonprofit experts; and identify areas for improvement in supporting preparedness and recovery.
