bills.title: Fiscal Year 2024 Veterans Affairs Major Medical Facility Authorization Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This law, titled the "Fiscal Year 2024 Veterans Affairs Major Medical Facility Authorization Act" (Public Law 118-124), authorizes the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to carry out a specified set of major medical facility construction and renovation projects during fiscal year 2024. It also authorizes funding for those projects.

The law identifies eleven specific projects, each at a named location with a maximum dollar amount that cannot be exceeded:

- American Lake, Washington — new specialty care building, renovation, and parking: up to $155.6 million.
- Dallas, Texas — mental health clinical space, parking, and land acquisition: up to $93.1 million.
- El Paso, Texas — new health care center and utility plant: up to $759.2 million.
- Perry Point, Maryland — community living center replacement and parking: up to $274.31 million.
- Portland, Oregon — seismic retrofit, renovations, new specialty care facility, and parking: up to $613 million.
- Reno, Nevada — initiation of medical center replacement, including land acquisition: up to $223.8 million.
- San Diego, California — new spinal cord injury building, renovations, parking, and seismic retrofit: up to $311.7 million.
- San Francisco, California — new research facility, parking, and demolition: up to $264.5 million.
- San Juan, Puerto Rico — seismic corrections, new administrative building, and outpatient clinic expansion: up to $370.37 million.
- St. Louis, Missouri — Phase 1 of bed tower replacement and related work: up to $135.34 million.
- West Haven, Connecticut — new surgical and clinical tower, renovations, and demolition: up to $153.128 million.

The law authorizes appropriations of $3,354,048,000 to the Secretary for fiscal year 2024 (or the year in which funds are appropriated) under the Construction, Major Projects account, to fund these authorized projects.

The Act was approved on November 25, 2024.

bills.title: COCOA Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Confirmation Of Congressional Observer Access Act of 2024 (COCOA Act of 2024) amends the Help America Vote Act of 2002 to require states to grant access to designated congressional election observers during federal elections.

The law adds a new Section 304 to Title III of the Help America Vote Act. It begins by stating Congress's position that, under Article I of the Constitution, it already has the authority to send observers to polling locations and any location where votes are processed, scanned, tabulated, canvassed, recounted, audited, or certified. The bill states that it does not establish new authorities but provides a statutory reference for existing congressional activity.

Under the new provision, states must give each designated congressional election observer full access to observe all elements of election administration, including areas where ballots are cast, processed, tabulated, recounted, audited, or certified, including pre- and post-election procedures. Observers may ask questions of election officials and workers.

The bill restricts observer conduct: observers may not handle ballots or election equipment, advocate for any candidate or position, reduce ballot secrecy or voter privacy, or interfere with voters or election administrators.

State or local election officials may remove an observer if they have a reasonable basis to believe the observer has engaged in or will imminently engage in intimidation, deceptive practices prohibited by federal law, or disruption of voting or ballot processing. The mere presence of an observer is not sufficient grounds for removal. If an observer is removed, the official must, within 24 hours, notify the chair and ranking minority member of the House Committee on House Administration or the Senate Committee on Rules and Administration and provide written reasons. The relevant committee leaders may send a replacement observer.

A "designated congressional election observer" is a House or Senate employee designated in writing by the chair or ranking minority member of the relevant committee. "State" includes the 50 states, the District of Columbia, and listed U.S. territories.

The Act also makes conforming enforcement and clerical amendments. It was approved October 4, 2024.

accurate.why: Accurately describes HAVA Section 304 observer access, restrictions, removal, and definitions.

states_purpose.why: States purpose to require states grant observer access.

exceptions_conditions.why: Covers conduct restrictions and removal conditions including 'mere presence' rule.

effective_dates.why: Notes 24-hour notice deadline and approval date.

correct_entities.why: HAVA, committees correct.

openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The COCOA Act of 2024 amends the Help America Vote Act of 2002 to require states to provide access for designated congressional election observers during elections for Federal office.

The Act adds a new section 304 to Title III of the Help America Vote Act. It states Congress’s finding that it has constitutional authority to send observers to observe polling places and other parts of the federal election process, including processing, scanning, tabulating, canvassing, recounting, auditing, and certifying voting results. It also states that the new statutory language does not create new congressional authority but provides a statutory reference for existing authority and activity.

States must give each designated congressional election observer full access to clearly observe all elements of election administration procedures, including areas where ballots are cast, processed, scanned, tabulated, canvassed, recounted, audited, or certified, and including pre-election and post-election procedures.

Observers may not handle ballots or election equipment, advocate for a candidate or position, reduce ballot secrecy or voter privacy, interfere with a voter’s ability to cast a ballot, interfere with an election administrator’s duties, or otherwise interfere with the election process. The Act clarifies that observers are not prohibited from asking questions of election administrators, officials, workers, or other state or local officials.

A state or local election official may remove an observer if the official has a reasonable basis to believe the observer has engaged in or is about to engage in intimidation, deceptive practices prohibited by federal law, or disruption of election procedures. Mere presence is not sufficient grounds for removal. Within 24 hours after removal, the official must notify the appropriate congressional committee leaders in writing and explain the reasons. A replacement observer may be sent.

A designated congressional election observer must be a House or Senate employee designated in writing by specified committee leaders. The Act applies to the 50 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, the U.S. territories, and the Northern Mariana Islands. It also updates related enforcement and table-of-contents provisions.

accurate.why: Accurate across access, restrictions, removal, and definitions.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Notes new section 304 and conforming amendments.

exceptions_conditions.why: Covers restrictions and removal conditions.

correct_entities.why: HAVA and committees correct.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The "Confirmation Of Congressional Observer Access Act of 2024" (or "COCOA Act of 2024") amends the Help America Vote Act of 2002 to require states and U.S. territories to provide designated congressional election observers with full access to observe election administration procedures during federal elections. 

The bill establishes a statutory reference for Congress's constitutional authority to send observers to polling places and any location where ballots are cast, processed, scanned, tabulated, canvassed, recounted, audited, or certified. A "designated congressional election observer" is defined as a House or Senate employee designated in writing by the chair or ranking minority member of the House Committee on House Administration or the Senate Committee on Rules and Administration. 

Under the law, observers are permitted to ask questions of election officials and workers. However, they are strictly prohibited from handling ballots or election equipment, advocating for candidates or positions, compromising voter privacy, or interfering with voters or election administrators. 

A state or local election official may remove an observer if there is a reasonable basis to believe the observer is disrupting procedures or engaging in illegal intimidation or deceptive practices; however, the mere presence of an observer is not a valid reason for removal. If an observer is removed, the election official must notify the relevant congressional committee's chair and ranking minority member in writing within 24 hours, detailing the reasons for the removal. The committee leadership may then send a replacement observer for the remainder of the election observation.

The Act adds a new Section 304 to Title III of the Help America Vote Act, which affirms Congress's constitutional authority to send observers to polling locations and other areas where election processes occur.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The Confirmation Of Congressional Observer Access Act of 2024 (also called the COCOA Act) amends the Help America Vote Act of 2002 to require states to give designated congressional election observers full access to monitor federal election procedures. The law states that Congress possesses constitutional authority to send such observers and establishes a statutory framework for that access.

Under the new provisions, each state must allow designated observers to clearly observe all elements of election administration, including any location where ballots are cast, processed, scanned, tabulated, canvassed, recounted, audited, or certified—as well as pre- and post-election activities. Observers are subject to specific restrictions: they may not handle ballots or election equipment, advocate for any position or candidate, reduce ballot secrecy or voter privacy, interfere with voters or election administrators, or otherwise disrupt the process. They are permitted to ask questions of election officials and other state or local personnel.

The law also sets out a removal process. If a state or local election official has a reasonable basis to believe an observer has engaged in or is about to engage in intimidation, deceptive practices prohibited by federal law, or disruption of voting, processing, tabulating, canvassing, or certification, the official may remove that observer. Mere presence, without additional evidence, is not a sufficient reason for removal. Within 24 hours of removal, the official must notify the chair and ranking minority member of the relevant congressional committee (House Administration or Senate Rules and Administration) and provide written reasons. If an observer is properly removed, the committee chair or ranking minority member may send a replacement observer.

A “designated congressional election observer” is defined as a House or Senate employee designated in writing by the chair or ranking minority member of those committees to gather information, including for purposes related to contested elections and other constitutional functions. The term “State” includes the 50 states, the District of Columbia, and U.S. territories. The Act also makes conforming amendments to enforcement and table-of-contents sections of the Help America Vote Act. It took effect upon enactment on October 4, 2024.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Confirmation Of Congressional Observer Access Act of 2024 or the COCOA Act of 2024
This act provides statutory authority for designated congressional election observers. Designated congressional election observer refers to an employee of the House of Representatives or the Senate who is designated in writing by the chair or ranking minority member of the House Committee on House Administration or the Senate Committee on Rules and Administration to gather information with respect to an election, including in the event that the election is contested in the House or the Senate and for other purposes permitted by Article I, Section 5, clause 1 and Article I, Section 4, clause 1 of the U.S. Constitution.
The act requires states to provide designated congressional election observers with full access to clearly observe all elements of administration procedures of federal elections, including access to any area in which a ballot is cast, processed, scanned, tabulated, canvassed, recounted, audited, or certified, including during pre- and post-election procedures.
The act prohibits such an observer from handling ballots or election equipment, advocating for a position or candidate, taking any action to reduce ballot secrecy or voter privacy, taking any action to interfere with the ability of a voter to cast a ballot or an election administrator to carry out the administrator's duties, or otherwise interfering with the election administration process.
The act specifies the conduct for which designated congressional election observers may be removed by election officials. In the event of removal, the election official must, within 24 hours of the observer's removal, provide written notice to the chair and ranking minority member of the House Committee on House Administration or the Senate Committee on Rules and Administration.

bills.title: HEARTS Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The HEARTS Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-176) amends the Public Health Service Act to expand federal efforts on cardiomyopathy education, awareness, research, and the availability of automated external defibrillators (AEDs) and CPR training in schools. It adds four new sections (312A–312D) to the Act.

**Education materials (Section 312A):** Within 18 months of enactment, the Secretary of Health and Human Services, in consultation with the CDC Director, must develop public education materials covering: signs, symptoms, and risk factors of high-risk cardiac conditions (including cardiomyopathy, long QT syndrome, and others) that may cause sudden cardiac arrest in young people; guidelines for AED placement in schools and childcare settings; AED and CPR training information; and recommendations for cardiac emergency response plans. Within 30 months, the Secretary must disseminate these materials through state educational agencies, health departments, health professionals, and the CDC website.

**Cardiomyopathy activities (Section 312B):** Within 18 months, the Secretary, through the CDC, must report to Congress on existing findings about the prevalence and epidemiology of cardiomyopathy, including related emergency visits and hospitalizations. The report must be public while complying with privacy laws. The Secretary must also develop a publicly available cardiomyopathy risk assessment for providers and individuals, with input from patient advocacy groups, medical societies, and federal agencies.

**Research (Section 312C):** The Secretary, with the NIH Director, may expand and coordinate NIH research on cardiomyopathy causes, outcomes, and treatments, avoiding duplication of other HHS efforts. Within 18 months, a report to Congress must outline ongoing research and identify research agendas and areas of greatest need.

**School AED/CPR grants (Section 312D):** The Secretary may award grants to local educational agencies (in consultation with qualified health care entities) to promote student access to AEDs and CPR in public schools, funding training, materials, emergency plans, and the purchase and maintenance of approved AEDs.

The Act authorizes no additional appropriations to carry out these provisions. It was approved December 23, 2024.

accurate.why: accurately covers sections 312A-312D

exceptions_conditions.why: notes no additional appropriations and grant eligibility

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The HEARTS Act of 2024 amends the Public Health Service Act to require federal education, awareness, risk assessment, reporting, research, and school preparedness activities related to cardiomyopathy and other high-risk cardiac conditions, and to authorize grants promoting access to automated external defibrillators (AEDs) and CPR in public schools.

The Act requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services, in consultation with the CDC Director, to develop public education materials within 18 months for school administrators, educators, school health professionals, coaches, families, caregivers, and others. These materials must cover signs, symptoms, and risk factors for cardiomyopathy and specified genetic heart rhythm abnormalities that may cause sudden cardiac arrest in children, adolescents, and young adults; guidelines for AED placement in schools, early childhood education programs, and child care centers; AED and CPR training information; and recommendations for cardiac emergency response plans. Within 30 months, the Secretary must disseminate these materials through state educational agencies, health departments, health professionals, and the CDC website.

The Act also requires the CDC, within 18 months, to report to Congress on existing activities concerning the prevalence and epidemiology of cardiomyopathy across the lifespan, including natural history and estimates of related emergency department visits and hospitalizations. The report must be public, subject to federal and state privacy laws. The Secretary must develop a publicly available cardiomyopathy risk assessment for health care providers and individuals, with stakeholder input.

The Act permits the Secretary, in consultation with NIH, to expand and coordinate cardiomyopathy research, while avoiding unnecessary duplication, and requires an NIH report to Congress within 18 months on ongoing research, research agendas, pediatric research plans, and areas of greatest need.

Finally, the Act authorizes grants to local educational agencies, in consultation with qualified health care entities, to support AED and CPR programs in public elementary and secondary schools, including training, emergency response plans, AED purchase and maintenance, and replacement of outdated equipment. It authorizes no additional appropriations for these activities.

bills.title: Holcombe Rucker Park National Commemorative Site Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Holcombe Rucker Park National Commemorative Site Act (Public Law 118-235) designates Holcombe Rucker Park in Harlem, New York, as the "Holcombe Rucker Park National Commemorative Site." The purpose of the designation is to memorialize the achievements of Holcombe Rucker and to recognize the historic role of the park in the development of the game of basketball and many of its players.

The law authorizes the Secretary of the Interior to enter into cooperative agreements with appropriate public or private entities—including the Mayor of New York City or the Mayor's designee—for interpretive and educational purposes related to the site. These activities may include installing a plaque, interpretive exhibit, or other marker, but only with the written consent of the property's owner.

The Act establishes several limitations. The Commemorative Site is not made a unit of the National Park System. Nothing in the law authorizes the Secretary of the Interior to interfere with private property rights or with any local zoning ordinance or land use plan of New York State or its political subdivisions, including actions a property owner may otherwise take. The law does not affect the administration of the site by New York City or New York State, and it does not authorize the Secretary to acquire land or interests in land through condemnation or any other means.

The Act was approved on January 4, 2025. It originated as H.R. 6852, passed the House on September 24, 2024, and passed the Senate on December 20, 2024.

accurate.why: accurately describes designation, cooperative agreements, and limitations

changes_to_existing_law.why: creates a designation; does not amend existing statutory law

exceptions_conditions.why: notes limitations: not NPS unit, no condemnation, no interference with property/zoning, written-consent condition

major_provisions_covered.why: covers designation, agreements, and all limitations

correct_entities.why: Secretary of the Interior, NYC Mayor, NY State correct

bills.title: Congenital Heart Futures Reauthorization Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Congenital Heart Futures Reauthorization Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-107) amends Section 399V-2 of the Public Health Service Act to extend and expand the federal government's national program for congenital heart disease research, surveillance, and awareness.

The bill's primary purpose is to reauthorize this program and to require new reporting and strategic planning related to congenital heart disease.

The Act makes three main changes. First, it adds a new requirement for the Secretary of Health and Human Services to issue a report to specified congressional committees (the House Committee on Energy and Commerce and the Senate Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions) within two years of enactment. This report must describe past and present federal activities to raise public awareness of congenital heart disease, assess efforts to educate and train health care providers, and describe the current U.S. workforce capacity of providers treating adult patients with the disease.

Second, the bill requires the Secretary, within one year after submitting that report, to develop and submit to Congress a strategy for improving public awareness and provider education and training. The strategy must include findings and recommendations to address gaps in public awareness and research regarding patients' lifelong needs (including long-term outcomes, quality of life, mental health, and health care utilization), address shortages in the workforce treating adult patients (potentially through fellowship or continuing education programs), and foster collaboration across federal agencies, providers, researchers, and patient organizations. In developing the strategy, the Secretary is to consult, as appropriate, with stakeholder groups such as patient organizations, health care professionals, research entities, insurers, accrediting organizations, and federal agencies including the CDC, NIH, and HRSA.

Third, the bill extends the program's authorization of appropriations, replacing the prior period of fiscal years 2020 through 2024 with fiscal years 2025 through 2029. It also redesignates existing subsections (f) and (g) as (g) and (h).

The Act was approved on November 21, 2024.

The Congenital Heart Futures Reauthorization Act of 2024 amends the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize the national congenital heart disease research, surveillance, and awareness program through fiscal years 2025 through 2029.

The Act adds new reporting and planning requirements for the Secretary of Health and Human Services. Within two years of enactment, the Secretary must submit a report to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce and the Senate Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. The report must describe past and current Department of Health and Human Services activities to increase public awareness and knowledge of congenital heart disease, including efforts addressing the lifelong needs of patients. It must also assess past and current Department activities to improve education and training of health care providers on congenital heart disease, including lifelong patient needs, and describe the current U.S. workforce capacity of providers who treat adults living with congenital heart disease.

Within one year after submitting that report, the Secretary must develop and submit to Congress a strategy to improve public awareness and knowledge, and provider education and training, related to congenital heart disease. The strategy must include findings and recommendations addressing public awareness and research gaps and opportunities related to lifelong patient needs, including long-term health outcomes, quality of life, mental health, and health care utilization. It must also address shortages in the workforce treating adult congenital heart disease patients, which may include ways to enhance fellowship training or continuing education, and promote collaboration and information sharing among federal agencies, health care providers, researchers, and patient organizations.

In developing the strategy, the Secretary must consult, as appropriate, with qualified stakeholders, including patient organizations, health care professionals, research entities, health insurers, accrediting organizations, and relevant federal agencies such as CDC, NIH, and HRSA.

bills.title: Prove It Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Prove It Act of 2024" (H.R. 7198) amends Chapter 6 of title 5 of the U.S. Code to increase transparency in federal regulatory decisions affecting small businesses under the Regulatory Flexibility Act.

The bill expands the required content of an agency's initial regulatory flexibility analysis (Section 603(b)) to include, where feasible, reasonably foreseeable indirect costs a proposed rule may impose on small entities—such as those that do business with directly regulated entities, are regulated by other governmental bodies because of the rule, or become subject to other agency rules as a result.

It creates a new petition process (new Section 605A). When an agency certifies that a proposed rule will not have a significant economic impact on a substantial number of small entities, small entities or organizations representing them may petition the Chief Counsel for Advocacy of the Small Business Administration to review that certification. Petitions must meet specified content requirements. Petitioners may request a consultation with the Chief Counsel, who is limited to procedural assistance and cannot advise on amending or withdrawing the petition.

The Chief Counsel conducts a prima facie review and, within 10 days, either closes the matter or proceeds to a full review. A full review involves examining the agency's analysis and convening a meeting with the petitioner, agency representatives, and the Administrator of OIRA; results must be published within 30 days. If the Chief Counsel finds a significant economic impact, the agency must perform initial and final regulatory flexibility analyses. If an agency fails to participate in the review, the final rule will not apply to small entities. Certain certifications are treated as final agency action for judicial review purposes.

The bill also requires agencies to publish guidance documents on regulations.gov and allow comment on them (Section 609). It expands periodic review of rules (Section 610) to include indirect costs, and provides that rules not reviewed within the required 10-year period cease to be effective unless reinstated after public comment and completed review within 180 days. This provision applies to final rules issued during the five years before enactment or afterward.

The bill passed the House on December 5, 2024.

accurate.why: all claims supported by bill text or accurate context

changes_to_existing_law.why: describes amendments to ch.6 title 5 (RFA): secs 603, 605, 605A, 609, 610

exceptions_conditions.why: notes 'where feasible' indirect costs, agency-nonparticipation penalty, reinstatement conditions

correct_entities.why: Chief Counsel for Advocacy/SBA, OIRA, regulations.gov correct

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Prove It Act of 2024 amends the Regulatory Flexibility Act in title 5 of the U.S. Code to increase procedures and disclosures related to federal rules that may affect small entities.

For an initial regulatory flexibility analysis, the bill requires agencies, where feasible, to include reasonably foreseeable indirect costs a proposed rule may impose on small entities, including those that do business with directly regulated entities, are regulated by other governmental entities because of the rule, or become subject to other agency rules because of the rule. It also requires an agency that certifies a proposed rule will not have a significant economic impact on a substantial number of small entities to publish that certification within 10 days after completing it.

The bill creates a process for small entities, groups of small entities, or organizations representing them to petition the Chief Counsel for Advocacy of the Small Business Administration to review such certifications. Petitions must include specified identifying information, objections to the certification, supporting data, and proposed solutions. The Chief Counsel must decide within 10 days whether the petition merits further review. If it does, the Chief Counsel must conduct a full review, including a meeting with the petitioner, the agency, and the Administrator of the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs, and publish results within 30 days. If the Chief Counsel finds the rule would have a significant economic impact on a substantial number of small entities, the agency must prepare initial and final regulatory flexibility analyses. If an agency fails to attend the meeting or otherwise assist the review, the final rule does not apply to small entities. The bill also specifies when such certifications become final agency actions for judicial review.

For rules likely to have a significant economic impact on a substantial number of small entities, agencies must publish guidance and other relevant documents, including updates, on regulations.gov or a similar website and allow public comments.

The bill also expands the factors agencies must consider in the required 10-year review of rules affecting small entities to include indirect costs. If an agency fails to complete that review, the Chief Counsel must notify the agency that the rule has ceased to be effective, and the agency must publish notice and seek comments on reinstatement. If reinstatement is warranted, the agency has 180 days to complete the review, after which the rule is reinstated without separate notice-and-comment rulemaking. This enforcement provision applies to final rules issued within five years before enactment or on or after enactment.

This includes costs on small entities that do business with directly regulated entities or are indirectly affected by the rule. It also mandates that agencies publish certifications regarding a rule's economic impact within 10 days of completion.

The legislation establishes a new review process allowing small entities or their representatives to petition the Small Business Administration’s Chief Counsel for Advocacy to review an agency's certification that a proposed rule will not significantly impact small businesses. The Chief Counsel must conduct an initial review within 10 days and, if warranted, a full review within 30 days, which includes a meeting with the petitioning entity, the agency, and the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs. If an agency fails to participate in this review, the final rule will not apply to small entities. The bill also makes such certifications subject to judicial review.

Additionally, the bill requires agencies to publish guidance documents for rules significantly impacting small entities on regulations.gov and allow public comment. 

Finally, it modifies the 10-year periodic review of existing rules to include indirect costs. If an agency fails to complete this review, the rule ceases to be effective, and the agency must solicit public comments on whether to reinstate it. If reinstated, the agency has 180 days to complete the review, and the rule is reinstated without standard notice-and-comment rulemaking procedures. This provision applies to final rules issued within five years before the Act's enactment or on or after the date of enactment.

Prove It Act of 2024
This bill expands the requirements for federal agency rulemaking with respect to small businesses, organizations, and governmental jurisdictions.
Specifically, when conducting an initial regulatory flexibility analysis, agencies must include, where feasible, any reasonably foreseeable potential indirect costs the proposed rule may impose on such small entities.
Further, if an agency certifies that an initial regulatory flexibility analysis is not required because the rule will not have a significant economic impact on a substantial number of small entities, the agency must provide such certification within 10 days to the Office of Advocacy of the Small Business Administration. A small entity or group of small entities may petition the Office of Advocacy to review such certification. The petition must include specified information, such as the issues the petitioner believes should be addressed and a proposed solution to the issues raised.
If the Office of Advocacy ultimately determines, upon a full review of the petition, that the proposed rule would have a significant economic impact on a substantial number of small entities, the agency promulgating the rule must perform an initial and final regulatory flexibility analysis for the rule. Additionally, if the agency does not participate or assist in the full review process, the finalized rule shall not apply to small entities.
The bill also requires agencies to publish, and allow for comments on, all guidance documents with respect to any rule an agency determines is likely to have a significant economic impact on a substantial number of small entities.

bills.title: Autism CARES Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Autism Collaboration, Accountability, Research, Education, and Support Act of 2024 (Autism CARES Act of 2024) amends the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize and expand federal activities and programs related to autism spectrum disorder (ASD). It became Public Law 118-180 on December 23, 2024.

**National Institutes of Health (NIH) activities.** The bill broadens the scope of autism research the NIH supports, adding fields such as psychiatry, psychology, developmental behavioral pediatrics, audiology, and gerontology. It directs that research address services and supports across the lifespan, examine supports for caregivers, and reflect the full population of individuals with ASD, including those with co-occurring conditions. It expands the number of authorized Centers of Excellence reviews, adds public input requirements, and requires the NIH Director to submit annual budget estimates for fiscal years 2026 through 2029 tied to the autism research strategic plan.

**Autism programs.** The bill extends the authorization of the developmental disabilities surveillance and research program, the autism education/early detection/intervention program, and the Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee from December 20, 2024, to September 30, 2029. It replaces terms like "culturally competent" with "culturally and linguistically appropriate," and "at higher risk" with "at increased likelihood." It strengthens early-detection program capacity, requires a report within two years on expanding developmental-behavioral pediatrician training, and revises intervention research provisions to focus on evidence-based practices across the lifespan.

**Reports and committee.** The Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee must develop a strategic plan with budgetary requirements and submit annual and biennial updates. The bill requires updated reports to Congress, including a report on mental health needs of individuals with ASD and, within two years, an update on young adults transitioning from school-based services to adult services.

**Funding.** It raises authorized appropriations for fiscal years 2025–2029: to $28,100,000, $56,344,000, and $306,000,000 for the respective programs.

**Communication tools.** Section 4 authorizes the Secretary of Health and Human Services to provide training and technical assistance, upon request, to states, tribes, localities, or territories regarding using federal funding for communication-supporting services and technologies, with annual reports to Congress.

The Autism CARES Act of 2024 amends the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize and revise federal activities related to autism spectrum disorder and other developmental disabilities through fiscal year 2029.

The Act expands National Institutes of Health autism research activities by requiring consultation with relevant federal agencies, adding fields such as psychiatry, psychology, developmental behavioral pediatrics, audiology, and gerontology, and specifying research on interventions, co-occurring conditions, caregiver supports, and the full range of support and service needs across the autism population. It extends the maximum support period for NIH autism centers of excellence from five to seven years, revises public input provisions, and requires NIH to submit annual budget estimates for autism initiatives for fiscal years 2026 through 2029.

The Act extends the developmental disabilities surveillance and research program, autism education and early detection activities, and the Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee through September 30, 2029. It revises education, detection, and intervention programs to emphasize culturally and linguistically appropriate information, screening and diagnostic tools, community-based research approaches, multidisciplinary and underserved-population experience, and partnerships with community-based organizations. It directs the Secretary of Health and Human Services to report within two years on the need for and feasibility of expanding developmental-behavioral pediatrician training programs.

The Act revises intervention research authority to support grants or contracts for evidence-based practices addressing physical health, behavioral health, and communication needs across the lifespan. It updates requirements for Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee strategic plans and annual and biennial updates.

It requires a federal progress report within four years after September 30, 2024, updates reporting on mental health needs, and requires within two years an update on youth transitioning to adulthood. It authorizes appropriations for fiscal years 2025 through 2029 of $28.1 million annually for surveillance, $56.344 million annually for education and early detection, and $306 million annually for NIH activities. It also authorizes HHS, upon request, to provide technical assistance on using federal funds for communication-related services, tools, and technologies, with annual reports to Congress.

The Autism Collaboration, Accountability, Research, Education, and Support Act of 2024 amends the Public Health Service Act to enhance and reauthorize federal activities and programs related to autism spectrum disorder through fiscal year 2029.

The Act expands National Institutes of Health (NIH) research activities to include fields such as psychiatry, psychology, developmental behavioral pediatrics, audiology, and gerontology. It requires research to examine supports for caregivers and reflect the entire autism population, including those with co-occurring conditions and a full range of support needs. It increases the number of Autism Centers of Excellence from five to seven and directs the NIH Director to facilitate public input and submit annual budget estimates for fiscal years 2026 through 2029.

The legislation reauthorizes the Developmental Disabilities Surveillance and Research Program, autism education and intervention programs, and the Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee through September 30, 2029. It updates terminology, replacing "culturally competent" with "culturally and linguistically appropriate" and "higher risk" with "increased likelihood." It also requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to submit a report within two years of enactment on the feasibility of expanding developmental-behavioral pediatrician training programs, and an update within two years on the transition challenges faced by young adults with autism. A broader progress report on autism activities is due within four years of September 30, 2024. The Act renames a report on "Health and Well-Being" to focus on "Mental Health Needs."

Additionally, the Act authorizes the HHS Secretary to provide technical assistance to states, Indian Tribes, localities, and territories on using federal funding for evidence-based communication tools for individuals with autism, accompanied by an annual report to Congress. It reauthorizes appropriations for these programs for fiscal years 2025 through 2029, increasing authorized funding levels.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The Autism Collaboration, Accountability, Research, Education, and Support Act of 2024 (Autism CARES Act of 2024) reauthorizes and expands federal autism spectrum disorder (ASD) programs through September 30, 2029. It amends the Public Health Service Act to broaden research, surveillance, education, and support activities, and increases authorized funding.

The act expands the scope of National Institutes of Health (NIH) autism research to include disciplines such as psychiatry, psychology, developmental behavioral pediatrics, audiology, and gerontology. Research must now address co-occurring conditions, caregiver supports, and the full range of needs across the lifespan. The number of NIH Centers of Excellence rises from five to seven. The NIH Director is required to provide public input opportunities and submit annual budget estimates for fiscal years 2026 through 2029.

Programs for developmental disabilities surveillance and autism education, early detection, and intervention are extended. Language is updated to require “culturally and linguistically appropriate” information and to refer to “increased likelihood” rather than “higher risk.” The act promotes community-based research, expands screening to include diagnostic tools, and strengthens state capacity to develop or expand programs with multidisciplinary, community-partnered approaches. The Secretary must report on the feasibility of expanding developmental-behavioral pediatrician training.

The Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee must meet regularly, develop a strategic plan with budget proposals, and submit annual and biennial updates to Congress. New reports are required: a progress report on ASD activities due within four years, a report on mental health needs of individuals with ASD, and an update on challenges faced by young adults transitioning to adulthood.

Authorized appropriations for fiscal years 2025 through 2029 are increased to $28.1 million for surveillance, $56.344 million for education and early detection, and $306 million for other autism-related activities. The act also allows the Secretary of Health and Human Services to provide technical assistance to states, tribes, and localities on using federal funds for communication tools, with an annual report to Congress.

bills.title: BOLD Infrastructure for Alzheimer's Reauthorization Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The BOLD Infrastructure for Alzheimer's Reauthorization Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-142) amends title III of the Public Health Service Act to extend funding for an existing federal program that promotes public health knowledge and awareness of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias.

Specifically, the law amends Section 398B(e) of the Public Health Service Act (42 U.S.C. 280c-5(e)) by adding an authorization of $33,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2025 through 2029. This continues funding for the program established under the original BOLD (Building Our Largest Dementia) Infrastructure for Alzheimer's framework.

The Act was approved on December 11, 2024. It originated as H.R. 7218, was passed by the House on September 17, 2024, and by the Senate on November 20, 2024.

The bill makes no other substantive changes to the underlying program's structure or operation; its sole effect is to extend and set the funding authorization level for the specified five-year period.

bills.title: Utah State Parks Adjustment Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Utah State Parks Adjustment Act (Public Law 118-181), enacted December 23, 2024, requires the federal government to transfer certain federal lands to the State of Utah for inclusion in three state parks.

The Act directs three conveyances, each to occur within 180 days of enactment, made subject to valid existing rights, without payment (no consideration), and by quitclaim deed:

1. **Antelope Island State Park:** The Secretary of the Interior must convey Bureau of Land Management (BLM) land identified on a map dated February 28, 2023.

2. **Wasatch Mountain State Park:** The Secretary of the Interior must convey BLM land identified on a map dated May 3, 2024.

3. **Fremont Indian State Park:** The Secretary of Agriculture must convey National Forest System land identified on a map dated September 12, 2023, along with any improvements on that land.

For all three conveyances, Utah must pay the associated costs, including surveys and other administrative expenses.

The Fremont Indian conveyance carries additional conditions. The Secretary of Agriculture must reserve easements for all National Forest System roads and trails that originate at, terminate at, or cross the conveyed land. The Secretary must convey only three specified water rights (numbered 63-44, 63-1607, and 63-2817) that serve the Castle Rock Campground and the Belknap Historic Guard Station interpretive site. If deemed necessary, the exact acreage and legal description will be set by a survey approved by the Secretary, with the State paying reasonable survey costs. The Secretary of Agriculture may enter agreements with the State on further terms, such as managing the Belknap site, roads and trails, continued livestock grazing, Forest Service access to retained water rights, and other management matters.

Either Secretary may make minor modifications to the applicable maps. The State must use all conveyed land for public purposes—including parks, campgrounds, recreation, and permitted livestock grazing. If any parcel ceases to be used for such purposes, it may, at the relevant Secretary's discretion, revert to the United States.

bills.title: Royalty Resiliency Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Royalty Resiliency Act (Public Law 118-81) amends Section 111(j) of the Federal Oil and Gas Royalty Management Act of 1982 to revise how the federal government manages royalties from oil and gas production under "units" and "communitization agreements" (arrangements that combine multiple leases or tracts for joint development).

The primary purpose is to establish a deadline for the Secretary of the Interior to determine how production is allocated among the parties in these agreements and to clarify how royalties are reported and paid while a determination is pending.

Under the revised provision, the Secretary must issue all determinations of production allocation for units and communitization agreements within 120 days of receiving a request. Until the Secretary issues a determination, the lessee (or its designee) must report and pay royalties on oil and gas production each month based on the terms of the proposed allocation. After the determination is issued, the lessee must correct any prior reports and royalty payments as necessary, completing those corrections no later than the end of the third month following the month in which it receives the determination.

The Act also addresses interest charges. Provided that royalties are paid fully and on time each month according to the proposed allocation, the Secretary must waive any interest otherwise due on the obligations covered by the determination, through the end of the third month following the month the lessee receives the determination.

The provision does not apply to unit or communitization agreements that contain Indian lands.

The Act was approved on September 20, 2024.

accurate.why: Sec 111(j) amendment, 120-day deadline, third-month correction/waiver, Indian-lands exclusion all match.

no_hallucinations.why: No fabrication; units/communitization explanation accurate context.

exceptions_conditions.why: Notes interest-waiver condition and Indian-lands exclusion.

correct_entities.why: Secretary of Interior, FOGRMA correct.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Royalty Resiliency Act amends the Federal Oil and Gas Royalty Management Act of 1982 to set procedures and deadlines for royalty reporting and payment when oil and gas production is subject to a unit or communitization agreement and the allocation of production has not yet been formally determined.

The Act requires the Secretary of the Interior to issue any determination of allocations of production for units and communitization agreements within 120 days after a request for the determination. Until the Secretary issues that determination, the lessee or the lessee’s designee must report and pay royalties for each production month according to the proposed allocation of production for the unit or communitization agreement.

After receiving the Secretary’s determination, the lessee or designee must make any needed corrections to royalty reports and payments no later than the end of the third month after the month in which the determination is received. If royalties have been paid fully and on time each month to all parties according to the proposed allocation, the Secretary must waive interest on obligations covered by the determination until that same correction deadline.

The Act does not apply to unit or communitization agreements that contain Indian lands. It does not specify a separate effective date.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Royalty Resiliency Act (Public Law 118-81) amends the Federal Oil and Gas Royalty Management Act of 1982 to establish clear timelines and procedures for managing royalties from oil and gas leases under unit or communitization agreements. 

Under the Act, the Secretary is required to issue all determinations of production allocations for these agreements within 120 days of receiving a request. Until the Secretary issues this determination, the lessee or their designee must report and pay royalties on oil and gas production for each month based on the terms of the proposed allocation. 

Once the Secretary's determination is issued, the lessee or designee must correct their reports and royalty payments as necessary. This correction must be completed no later than the end of the third month following the month in which they receive the determination. 

Additionally, the Act requires the Secretary to waive any interest due on obligations subject to the determination until the end of the third month after the lessee or designee receives the determination. This interest waiver is strictly conditional upon the lessee making full and timely monthly royalty payments to all parties in accordance with the proposed allocation terms. 

The Act explicitly excludes any unit or communitization agreements that contain Indian lands from these requirements and provisions.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Royalty Resiliency Act amends the Federal Oil and Gas Royalty Management Act of 1982 to improve the management of royalties from oil and gas leases. Specifically, the Act modifies the process for determining allocations of production for units and communitization agreements.

Under the new provisions, the Secretary must issue all determinations of allocations of production for these units and agreements within 120 days of receiving a request. While awaiting the Secretary's determination, the lessee or its designee is required to report and pay royalties on oil and gas production each month in accordance with the terms of the proposed allocation. Once the Secretary issues the determination, the lessee or its designee must correct any necessary reports and adjust the amount of royalties paid by the end of the third month following the month they receive the determination.

Additionally, the Act includes a condition for waiving interest on royalty obligations. Subject to the full and timely monthly payment of royalties to all parties according to the proposed allocation, the Secretary shall waive interest due on obligations subject to the determination until the end of the third month following the receipt of the determination. 

A material exception is provided: these new requirements do not apply to unit or communitization agreements that contain Indian lands. The Act was approved and became effective on September 20, 2024.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Royalty Resiliency Act
This act modifies the process under which oil and gas leaseholders who have entered into certain joint drilling agreements (i.e., a communitization agreement or a unit agreement, except agreements containing Indian lands) to drill wells on leased land pay royalties to the Department of the Interior under the Federal Oil and Gas Royalty Management Act of 1982.
Under current law, Interior must issue a determination of allocations of royalty payments for oil and gas production under a joint agreement within 120 days of a request for determination. Generally, the first leaseholder to drill must pay any royalties due to Interior for all oil and gas production on the land subject to the agreement until Interior determines the royalty allocations of each leaseholder. If Interior fails to issue the determination by that deadline, then it must waive interest due on royalty obligations until the end of the month following the month in which the determination was made.
Under the act, a leaseholder must pay royalties on oil and gas production based on the lessee's proposed allocation of production under the joint agreement until Interior issues a determination of royalty allocations. After Interior issues the determination, then the lessee must correct, if necessary, the amount of royalties paid by the end of the third month following the month in which the lessee received the determination from Interior. Subject to the full and timely payment of monthly royalties in accordance with the agreement, Interior must waive interest due on royalty obligations until the end of the third month.

bills.title: Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-41) is a short-term measure that extends expiring Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) authorizations, aviation-related funding, and certain taxes. Its primary purpose is to continue these programs for a brief period—generally through May 10 or May 11, 2024—pending longer-term reauthorization. Most provisions accomplish this by replacing prior expiration dates (commonly March 8 or March 9, 2024) with the new dates.

Title I addresses FAA programs. It authorizes approximately $2.04 billion for the airport improvement program for the period from October 1, 2023, through May 10, 2024, with obligation authority extending through September 30, 2024. For calculating funding apportionments during this period, the FAA Administrator must first compute amounts on an annualized basis as if $3.35 billion were available for fiscal year 2024, then reduce all apportionments and certain other amounts by 39 percent. Title I also extends project grant authority and numerous other authorities, including insurance authority, unmanned aircraft (UAS) test ranges and special UAS authorities, airport safety provisions, weather reporting programs, remote tower and other pilot programs, and various advisory committees. It sets new funding levels for FAA operations (about $7.26 billion), facilities and equipment (about $1.79 billion), research and development (about $155 million), and small community air service programs, including Essential Air Service (about $216 million).

Title II extends, through May 11, 2024, the authority to make expenditures from the Airport and Airway Trust Fund, and extends through May 10 or May 11, 2024, the taxes that fund it, including aviation fuel taxes, ticket taxes on persons and property, and taxes related to fractional ownership programs.

Title III extends, through May 11, 2024, counter-UAS authorities under the Homeland Security Act of 2002.

The Act was approved on March 8, 2024.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024 temporarily extends Federal Aviation Administration programs, aviation-related authorities, and Airport and Airway Trust Fund taxes and spending authority.

The Act amends title 49 to authorize Airport Improvement Program funding of $2.041 billion for the period from October 1, 2023, through May 10, 2024. Amounts made available may be obligated through September 30, 2024, subject to appropriations limitations, and remain available until expended. For apportionment calculations during that period, the FAA must calculate amounts as if fiscal year 2024 funding were $3.35 billion and then reduce specified apportionments and related amounts by 39 percent. The Act also extends airport project grant authority and related apportionment rules through May 10, 2024.

The Act extends numerous aviation authorities and programs, generally from March 8 or March 9, 2024, to May 10 or May 11, 2024. These include aviation insurance authority, unmanned aircraft test ranges and special UAS authorities, airport safety and airspace hazard mitigation enforcement, competitive access reporting, certain authorities for the Marshall Islands, Micronesia, and Palau, compatible land use planning, non-movement area surveillance, commercial space transportation “learning period” limits, Midway Island Airport authority, contract weather observers, remote tower and UAS remote identification pilot programs, aviation consumer protection and disability advisory committees, enhanced traffic services, and airport property redevelopment.

The Act provides temporary authorizations for FAA operations, air navigation facilities and equipment, research, engineering, and development, weather reporting programs, supplemental discretionary airport funds, Essential Air Service, and assistance for airports not receiving sufficient service, all for the period ending May 10, 2024.

It amends the Internal Revenue Code to extend Airport and Airway Trust Fund expenditure authority through May 11, 2024, and extends aviation fuel taxes, passenger and cargo ticket taxes, and fractional ownership program tax provisions generally through May 10 or May 11, 2024. It also extends Department of Homeland Security counter-unmanned aircraft system authorities through May 11, 2024.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024 temporarily extends the authorization of Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) programs and the taxes and expenditure authority that fund the Airport and Airway Trust Fund. The Act generally shifts the expiration dates of various expiring aviation programs, authorities, and taxes from March 8 or 9, 2024, to May 10 or 11, 2024.

Major provisions of the Act include:
* **Federal Aviation Funding:** Authorizes specific funding levels for the period beginning October 1, 2023, and ending May 10, 2024, including $2,041,120,218 for the Airport Improvement Program (AIP), $7,259,685,792 for FAA operations, $1,794,357,923 for air navigation facilities and equipment, and $155,368,852 for research, engineering, and development.
* **AIP Apportionment Conditions:** Requires the FAA to calculate AIP funding apportionments on an annualized basis of $3.35 billion and then reduce those calculated amounts by 39 percent for the designated period. AIP sums may be obligated through September 30, 2024.
* **Program Extensions:** Extends project grant authority, aviation insurance, unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) test ranges, and several advisory committees to May 10, 2024. It also increases funding for the Essential Air Service to $216,192,407 for the same period.
* **Aviation Revenue:** Extends the expenditure authority of the Airport and Airway Trust Fund to May 11, 2024, and extends associated fuel, ticket, and fractional ownership taxes to May 10 or 11, 2024.
* **Counter-UAS Authorities:** Extends the Department of Homeland Security's counter-UAS authorities to May 11, 2024.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024 extends federal aviation programs, funding authorities, and related taxes that were previously set to expire in March 2024. The Act primarily extends these provisions through May 10, 2024, or May 11, 2024, depending on the specific statute.

Under Title I, the Act authorizes specific funding levels for the Airport Improvement Program (AIP), Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) operations, air navigation facilities and equipment, and aviation research for the period of October 1, 2023, through May 10, 2024. For the AIP, it authorizes over $2 billion and directs the FAA Administrator to calculate funding apportionments on an annualized basis as if $3.35 billion were available for fiscal year 2024, followed by a 39 percent reduction. The Act also extends the authority to obligate these funds through September 30, 2024. Additionally, it extends various miscellaneous aviation authorities, including aircraft insurance, unmanned aircraft systems testing, and small community air service programs, to May 10 or 11, 2024, and provides corresponding funding extensions for these programs.

Title II amends the Internal Revenue Code to extend the expenditure authority of the Airport and Airway Trust Fund through May 11, 2024. It also extends the collection of aviation fuel taxes, ticket taxes, and taxes related to fractional ownership programs that fund the trust fund through May 10 or 11, 2024.

Title III extends counter-unmanned aircraft systems (Counter-UAS) authorities under the Homeland Security Act of 2002 through May 11, 2024. 

The Act was approved on March 8, 2024, and serves as a short-term extension of existing aviation laws and revenue measures.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill extends the funding and legal authorities for several Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) programs and the aviation taxes that support them. It continues these programs from their previous expiration in early March 2024 through May 10, 2024, or in some cases May 11, 2024.

The legislation amends authorizations for the Airport Improvement Program, setting its funding at approximately $2.04 billion for the period from October 1, 2023, through May 10, 2024. The bill directs the FAA to calculate funding apportionments for this period as if a full year’s total were $3.35 billion, then to reduce all state and grant apportionment amounts by 39 percent. These funds may be obligated through September 30, 2024. The law also provides specific funding amounts for FAA operations, air navigation facilities and equipment, and research and engineering, all for the same October-to-May period. Funding for essential air service and assistance for airports with insufficient service is similarly extended and increased to cover the longer timeframe.

A broad range of other aviation-related authorities are extended to the new May expiration dates. These include the FAA’s authority to provide aviation insurance, rules for unmanned aircraft test ranges and special unmanned aircraft systems, the competitive access reporting requirement, the Midway Island Airport provision, and various pilot programs and advisory committees, including those concerning aviation consumer protection, air travel for passengers with disabilities, and airport property redevelopment.

On the revenue side, the bill extends the authority to spend from the Airport and Airway Trust Fund and extends the aviation excise taxes that finance the fund. This includes taxes on aviation fuel, passenger tickets, and provisions related to fractional ownership programs. Additionally, the legislation extends the Department of Homeland Security’s counter-unmanned aircraft systems (Counter-UAS) authorities.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Airport and Airway Extension Act of 2024
This act temporarily extends specific Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) programs and activities through May 10, 2024.
TITLE I--FEDERAL AVIATION PROGRAMS
(Sec. 101) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the Airport Improvement Program (AIP). This program provides grants for planning, development, and noise compatibility projects at or associated with certain public-use airports.
(Sec. 102) This section extends through May 10, 2024, certain expiring Department of Transportation (DOT) and FAA authorities and programs.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority of DOT to provide insurance and reinsurance for the federal government for risks from operating an aircraft and providing related goods or services.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) Test Site Program, which provides verification of the safety of public and civil UAS, operations, and related navigation procedures before their integration into the national airspace system.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority of DOT to provide exemptions from some requirements for UAS by using a risk-based approach to determine if certain UAS may operate safely in the national airspace system.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's airport safety and airspace hazard mitigation and enforcement program to test and evaluate technologies or systems that detect and mitigate potential aviation safety risks posed by UAS.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, competitive access disclosure requirements for AIP grant applications for large and medium hub airports. Specifically, an airport must provide assurances to DOT that it will report if it has been unable to accommodate one or more requests by an air carrier for access to gates or other facilities at that airport in order to provide service or expanded service.
This section extends authorization through May 10, 2024, for AIP grants for airports located in the Republic of the Marshall Islands, the Federated States of Micronesia, and the Republic of Palau.
This section extends authorization through May 10, 2024, for the FAA AIP supplementary discretionary funds for grants for airport or terminal development projects.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, AIP grant authorization for compatible land use planning and projects by state and local governments. Airport-compatible land uses are those that can coexist with a nearby airport without constraining the safe and efficient operation of the airport, or exposing people living or working nearby to significant environmental impacts.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, authorization for the AIP non-movement area surveillance surface display systems pilot grant program. These systems use on-airport sensors to track vehicles or aircraft that are equipped with transponders on portions of the airfield surface that are not under the control of air traffic control.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, authorization for the FAA weather reporting programs.
This section extends to May 11, 2024, a learning period (i.e., moratorium) that applies to certain DOT commercial spaceflight safety regulations governing the design or operation of a launch vehicle to protect the health and safety of crew, government astronauts, and space flight participants.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, authority for DOT to enter into a reimbursable agreement with the Department of Interior for the purpose of funding airport development at Midway Island Airport.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the termination date for a final order establishing mileage and adjustment eligibility under the Essential Air Service (EAS) Program using the most commonly used route between the place and the nearest medium hub airport or large hub airport. (The EAS program was established to ensure that small communities that were served by certificated air carriers before the Airline Deregulation Act of 1978 would continue to receive scheduled passenger service.)
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the moratorium on discontinuing the FAA Contract Weather Observer Program at any airport.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA remote tower pilot program. A remote tower is a remotely operated air navigation facility, including all necessary system components, that provides the functions and capabilities of an air traffic control tower whereby air traffic services are provided to operators at an airport from a location that may not be on or near the airport.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for DOT to provide AIP grants for airport access roads in certain remote locations and for storage facilities for certain snow removal and safety equipment.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA's UAS remote detection and identification pilot program. Under the program, the FAA uses available remote detection or identification technologies for safety oversight, including enforcement actions against operators of UAS that are not in compliance with federal aviation laws and regulations.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the Aviation Consumer Protection Advisory Committee, which advises DOT in carrying out activities relating to airline customer service improvements.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the requirement that the Aviation Consumer Advocate at DOT submit an annual report to Congress summarizing aviation consumer complaints and recommendations.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA Air Carrier Access Act Advisory Committee to address the needs of passengers with disabilities.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for an FAA pilot program to provide air traffic control services on a preferential basis to aircraft equipped with certain Next Generation Air Transportation System (NextGen) avionics.
This section extends through May 10, 2024, the pilot program that allows AIP funds to be used for activities related to the redevelopment of airport properties.
(Sec. 103) This section reauthorizes through May 10, 2024, FAA salaries, operations, and maintenance.
Further, this section extends through May 10, 2024, DOT's authority to reduce the FAA's expenses for non-safety-related activities if DOT determines that the funding levels are insufficient to meet the FAA's authorized salary, operations, and maintenance expenses.
(Sec. 104) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's authority to acquire, establish, and improve air navigation facilities and equipment.
(Sec. 105) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's authority to conduct civil aviation research, engineering, and development.
(Sec. 106) This section reauthorizes through May 10, 2024, the FAA Essential Air Service Program.
Further, this section reauthorizes through May 10, 2024, the Small Community Air Service Development Program, a DOT grant program designed to help small communities address air service and airfare issues.
TITLE II--AVIATION REVENUE PROVISIONS
(Sec. 201) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the FAA's authority for expenditures from the Airport and Airway Trust Fund (AATF). The AATF is the primary funding source for all major FAA accounts that fund federal aviation programs, with the remainder coming from general fund appropriations.
(Sec. 202) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority for the FAA to collect various taxes and fees to fund the AATF, including taxes on aviation fuel and airline tickets.
TITLE III--MISCELLANEOUS EXTENSIONS
(Sec. 301) This section extends through May 10, 2024, the authority of the Department of Homeland Security and the Department of Justice to take certain actions to mitigate a credible threat to certain facilities or assets from an unmanned aircraft system (UAS). These include certain facilities that are located in the United States and identified as high-risk and a potential target for unlawful UAS activity.

bills.title: Veterans’ Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2024

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Veterans' Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-130) directs the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to increase the rates of certain benefits paid to veterans with service-connected disabilities and to survivors of certain disabled veterans, effective December 1, 2024.

The bill requires the Secretary to raise the dollar amounts that were in effect on November 30, 2024, for the following benefits:
- Wartime disability compensation (under section 1114 of title 38, U.S. Code);
- Additional compensation for dependents (section 1115(1));
- The clothing allowance (section 1162);
- Dependency and indemnity compensation paid to surviving spouses (section 1311, subsections (a) through (d)); and
- Dependency and indemnity compensation paid to children (sections 1313(a) and 1314).

The size of the increase is tied to the cost-of-living adjustment applied to Social Security benefits. Specifically, each covered amount is to be increased by the same percentage as the increase in Social Security benefits under title II of the Social Security Act that takes effect December 1, 2024, as determined under section 215(i) of that Act.

The Act also permits the Secretary to administratively adjust, consistent with these increases, the disability compensation rates payable to certain persons under section 10 of Public Law 85-857 who have not received compensation under chapter 11 of title 38.

The Secretary is required to publish the adjusted amounts in the Federal Register no later than the date by which the corresponding Social Security cost-of-living figures must be published for fiscal year 2025.

The law does not specify a fixed percentage increase itself; it links the increase to the Social Security adjustment. It was approved November 25, 2024.

openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Veterans’ Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2024 requires the Department of Veterans Affairs to increase certain veterans’ and survivors’ benefit amounts effective December 1, 2024. The increase applies to the dollar amounts in effect on November 30, 2024, and must match the same percentage increase applied to Social Security title II benefits effective December 1, 2024, based on the cost-of-living adjustment determination under the Social Security Act.

The benefits subject to the increase are wartime disability compensation rates under 38 U.S.C. 1114; additional disability compensation paid for dependents under 38 U.S.C. 1115(1); the clothing allowance under 38 U.S.C. 1162; dependency and indemnity compensation for surviving spouses under 38 U.S.C. 1311(a) through (d); and dependency and indemnity compensation for children under 38 U.S.C. 1313(a) and 1314.

The Act also authorizes the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to make corresponding administrative adjustments to disability compensation rates for certain persons covered by section 10 of Public Law 85-857 who have not received compensation under chapter 11 of title 38.

The Secretary of Veterans Affairs must publish the adjusted benefit amounts in the Federal Register by the same deadline applicable to publication of the Social Security cost-of-living adjustment information for fiscal year 2025.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Veterans' Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2024 increases the rates of compensation for veterans with service-connected disabilities and the rates of dependency and indemnity compensation for the survivors of certain disabled veterans. 

Effective December 1, 2024, the Act directs the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to increase the benefit amounts in effect on November 30, 2024, for wartime disability compensation, additional compensation for dependents, clothing allowances, and dependency and indemnity compensation for surviving spouses and children. Each of these dollar amounts must be increased by the same percentage as the cost-of-living adjustment applied to Social Security benefits under title II of the Social Security Act, effective December 1, 2024. 

Additionally, the Act contains a special rule allowing the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to administratively adjust the compensation rates for certain individuals under section 10 of Public Law 85-857 who do not receive compensation under chapter 11 of title 38, United States Code, consistent with these increases. 

The Secretary is required to publish the adjusted rates in the Federal Register. This publication must occur no later than the date on which the Social Security Administration is required to publish its own benefit adjustments determined during fiscal year 2025.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Describes increases.

exceptions_conditions.why: No material exceptions; special rule noted.

correct_entities.why: VA, SS Act, PL 85-857 correct.
